skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: A shallow tray or dish used for baking food.
Containers identified by the form they take rather than by function.
A drinking vessel, usually curved in shape, fashioned out of animal tusk or horn and sometimes with bands of decoration.

A coin which has been struck in relief on one side, usually the obverse side face down on the anvil die, and incuse on the other side, due to a previously struck coin sticking to the upper or punch die.
A piece of metal, usually cast, struck or stamped, with a definite value.
A small open container, usually with one handle, used for drinking.
A cup with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A container used to serve drinks and liquids.
A container used to assist in cooling water.
A container used to assist in cooling wine.
A container designed to assist in cooling foodstuffs or liquids. Use specific type where known.
One of a pack, or deck, of cards used to play games, each distinguished from the others by markings on its face.
A multi sided object usually used to obtain a unique value in games of chance.
A heavy disc, usually thrown.
An item designed to be played with by children.
A peg or upright object which is the target of a ball in skittles.
A toy designed to exercise the ingenuity.
A gaming implement usually used in conjunction with a ball.
A small ball, usually of glass or porcelain, used in games.
A flat, oblong gaming piece marked with spots used to play dominoes.
A metal spur fitted to the leg of a fighting cock as an aid in fighting.
A container in which dice are shaken and from which dice are thrown.
A marker used in the playing of a game.
A ring, often metal, which is used to be thrown as part of a game.
A round object used in games.
A marker used to represent a gambling stake.
A board marked up to play a game upon.
Objects used or associated with sport and games, eg gaming piece. This class includes toys.
Parts which make up a vehicle.
Usually made of iron, this bound the end of an axle and was perforated to allow a linch pin to pass through the axle and keep the wheel in place.
A container used in the preparartion of food.
A mixing or grinding vessel.
Use for deposits representing material discarded or remaining as a result of a process or processes.

Use for any soil or sediment. Sediments consist of mineral and/or organic material deposited by any process involving natural or human agencies. Soils are discrete bodies of altered mineral material (either sediment or in situ rock remains).

A jar-shaped vessel with pierced apertures or cut-out on an open or closed pedestal base. Herbs, spices or scented woods would be placed in the pot and a burning coal placed underneath to warm the herbs so they would release their scent.
A pouch for containing tobacco.
An object belonging to, used by, or related exclusively to one person and normally carried or kept privately by that person.
Worked or unworked block/boulder/cobble, on which grain was ground by to-and-fro motion, resulting in a deep smooth elongated hollowed area.
A stone with a concave upper surface on which grain was ground by using a rubbing stone.
A stone for grinding grain.
A quern where one stone is rotated on another to grind grain.
A method of grinding cereals where one stone is rotated on another by hand.
Equipment used in the grinding of cereal to produce foodstuffs.
An outer garment worn on the legs.
A piece of cloth or plastic worn especially by babies to protect clothes while eating.
A shirt like garment.
An opaque, rigid sheath used to cover the penis. Not usually contraceptive and not for obvious visual display.
A long loose fitting garment worn instead of a coat.
Clothes worn so that they are visible, usually but not always worn over other clothes.
A voluminous garment worn over other clothing.
A garment covering from the waist to the knees, similar to trousers but stop at the knees.
A sleeved garment worn on the upper body.
A long outer garment reaching the ankles.
An appendage worn on the front of breeches.
A loose piece of clothing about hip length worn over other clothes, not usually tucked in at the waist.
A protective outer garment.
Outerwear covering the upperbody, including the arms. Covers to the waist.
A piece of material worn over the head and or shoulders.
Outerwear designed to protect the legs from weather.
A garment worn from the waist down by women.
Pleated skirt, usua;;y of a tartan material, worn by men and women.
An African cloth garment.
A garment worn by females.
An underskirt or dress with little shaping.
Clothing worn on the upper body.
Covering and accessories worn on the body.
Cloth or leather covering for the leg or ankle.
Sleeveless short jacket.
A sleeved outer garment.
A piece of cloth worn around the loins.
A garment used to wrap around a person.
Remains of the animal group characterized by having jointed limbs and segmented bodies, including woodlice, spiders, insects etc. Note, crustaceans have been included in this category.
Use for the remains of any part of an insect.
An object to fasten, bind or join one item to another.
A metal fastener used to hold a tile or stone against a spacer and driven into a wall thus forming a flue between the wall and the tile or stone. Also used to hold timber together.
A tool, piece of equipment, container or other object used for or in fishing.
An object used to catch fish. The line is tied in the middle of the fish gorge which then lodges in the throat after the fish has swallowed it.
A container, usually metal, used to water plants.
A container in which plants are grown.
A container used in growing plants, either to contain or for use in their cultivation eg watering cans.
Containers identified by function rather than by form.
A container, usually ceramic, used to water plants.
A container for holding or pouring liquids, usually having a handle or lip.

A jug that incorporates a practical joke or conundrum into its construction. A combination of cut-outs in the body and hollow handles and tubes render conventional use impossible. The puzzle being to work out how to empty it. (Source: MPRG)
A hanging frame with branches which act as candle holders or to which lights are attached.
An object in which a candle is put in order to provide light.
Remains of one of the groups of animals characterized as lacking any form of backbone or spinal column.

Remains of animals characterized as having bodies made up of annular segments, such as worms and leeches. Earthworm eggs and granule comonly occur. Bristle worms are found associated with marine molluscs.
An object constructed to allow birds to bathe in it.
A piece of cast metal obtained from a mould in a form suitable for storage.
A piece of material that has been inlaid, or prepared and shaped for inlaying, in an object, decorative feature or other setting.
A clip for marking or holding down pages in a book.
An object that commemorates an animal, person, location or event.
A container holding items associated with the period of burial. Often buried to commemorate a certain event, such as the foundation of a building.
Use specific type where known, eg picture frame.
A pointed spout attachment.
Several strands of string twisted together to form a strong thin rope, or thick string, though it has been used in the past as synonomous with rope particularly for ropes on ships.
A collection of associated artefacts recovered from a single site.
The purpose of this object is unknown though it could be for surveying or it could be a charm.
An object marked or shaped by the pressure of another object upon its surface, whether by deliberate process or unintentionally.
A deliberate miniaturised replica of an object.
A long usually cylindrical object usually made of wood.
A box with one transparent side within which a picture, jewel etc can be displayed.
Coiled metal used to give or limit motion.
An item that cannot be identified. Use more specific term, if possible.
An unhewn piece of felled tree, or similar rough mass of wood.
A object fitted to an item or garment for the purpose of display or decoration.
Woven material.
An object showing signs of being worked. Use more specific term where known.
A rock carving, especially a prehistoric one.
Objects which cannot be assigned to any one particular class, or the function of which is unknown.
A shaped piece of wood that makes up a barrel, key etc.
A portable object of stone, bone, metal or other material featuring a deliberately engraved or incised design.
A projection which can be used to lift or suspend an object, often used as a handle.
A piece of openwork fabric made from twine or cord, either natural fibre or artificial, forming a mesh used for catching birds, animals or fish or for holding other inanimate objects.

Ammunition from any period found to have been expended (cannonballs, shells, bullets) which may indicate the possible site of a battle or action on land or at sea. Index where appropriate with Battlefield Debris as a Monument Type.
A pebble or small cobble with a small circular depression in one or both faces. The depressions, if on both faces, are usually directly opposite. These items may be unfinished pebble hammers.
A block on which objects are chopped or cut.
An object of Bronze Age date, usually of gold, with an unknown function.
A ball of chalk found on Neolithic sites.
A fragment of a vessel, or hollow object, usually of ceramic or glass. If known, use specific vessel type.
An object used for counting, keeping count or marking the position of a player in a game, usually in the form of a small circular-shaped piece of bone, metal, wood, plastic or other material.
Flint which has been burnt. It is not necessarily worked.
A metal collar or cap to strengthen the end of tubes, sticks, shafts, etc. Use with specific object name where known eg spear and ferrule.
An object which has one or more holes through it. Use more specific term where known.
The point or tine of a fork.
A piece of pointed wood, usually to be driven into the ground.
An inscribed tablet.
Use specific type where known.
A device controlling the passage of liquids or gases.
An object from which clothes can be hung.
A device to limit the amount of liquid coming from a pipe or hole.
Loose fibres obtained by unpicking old ropes. Mainly used to caulk seams in wooden vessels.
A rod, often of antler, with a hole through it and decorated.
Penannular objects of late Bronze Age, usually gold and too small to be worn on arms.
A portable shelter, usually of fabric or leather.
The part of an object by which it is held when in use, being moved or carried.
A series of connecting metal links.
Use specific form where known.
A tubular orifice to assist in pouring.
A cover for an object, usually designed to fit the object.

A slender, cylindrical object. Use specific types of rod where possible. Where it is a rod of material which is part of a production process, eg a glass rod, use rod and enter the material in an object material field.
A metal thread.
A flat, circular object often made from metal, wood, bone or ivory and of unidentified purpose.
A three dimensional representation of an object, person or animal.
A wheel with a series of teeth on its outside which interlock with similar teeth on another cogwheel to receive or give motion.
Any object which bears an inscription.
A plug for closing the neck of a container.
An ornamental tablet of brass. Use a materials thesaurus where brass is a material.
A length of cord usually made from twisting fibres together which increases the strength of the rope.
A collection of objects deliberately grouped together and hidden.
Pieces of undressed stone, waste fragments of stone or fragments of slate.
A sash or belt worn over the shoulder to the hip for carrying a sword.
An item worn as clothing or in addition to clothes.
A weapon for shooting arrows.
A large, powerful, hand-drawn bow, usually 6 ft (1.8m) in length and traditionally made of wood.
A pair of tongs used for placing coals onto a fire.
Tools used to maintain a fire.
A bowl used in the marriage ceremony.
A vessel in which incense is burnt.
A container used upon the altar.
Chests connected with the burial of a body.
A stemmed cup used for holding the wine during communion.
A container for holy relics.
A container for keeping the ashes of the dead after cremation.
A vessel for collecting tears.
A container with a religious, ritual or funerary use.
An accessory vessel to a cremation.
A basin of water used by priests for ritual ablutions.
A container for holding votive offerings.
A bowl used in magical ceremonies.
A container used to hold an offering.
A bowl used for divination.
A container within which a body is placed for burial, usually made of lead or wood.
A box to contain the dowry which is a gift or endowment given to or on behalf of the wife.
A bowl for the ritual washing of hands.
An Egyptian container for holding the entrails of a mummy.
A miniature lead phial worn around the neck as a religious pilgrim sign, generally medieval.
A container, in the form of an urn, for keeping the ashes of the dead after cremation.
A container for holding water that has been blessed by a priest and is used for symbolic rituals of purification.
A container used to hold and or pour out liquid in honour of a deity.
A vessel for keeping the ashes of the dead after cremation.
A vessel to contain the wafers used in the Eucharist.
An instrument for measuring the angle of slopes, usually in inches per yard, consisting of a weighted graduated wheel with movable sights.
Objects used to measure time, distance, weight, number or natural forces.
Containers in which food and or drink is gathered or processed.
A tray used for separating the grain from husks and straw.
Clothing worn on the foot.
A sole held onto the foot by straps or thongs.
A wide mouthed container, usually cylindrical without handles.

A jar with a profile that flares outwards from base to rim, but usually with a constriction at the neck. Squat examples, with a height less than the minimum diameter, could be classified as bowls. (Source: MPRG)
Ornament often with a hinged pin and catch, worn fastened to clothing.

Roman, mid-1st century AD two-piece Bow Brooch, derived from the Colchester Brooch, with a winged, transverse head protecting a spring mechanism held by a rear facing hook over the chord, its profile evoking a leaping dolphin.
Anglo-Saxon two-piece bow brooch, dating from the 8th to 9th centuries AD, so named for the distinctive bow shaped like the arching body of a caterpillar.

A two-piece Bow Brooch dating from the early to mid-first century AD in Britain, originating in Gaul. Featuring a wide flat reeded bow, this and the Thistle Brroch were the first to conceal the spring mechanism with a cylindrical or semi-cylindrical hood.
Any brooch with the pin sprung or hinged at one end and the catchplate at the other end of a curved or flat bow. Can be one or two piece construction.

Early to mid-1st century AD Bow Brooch with a P-shaped profile, a cylindrical or semi-cylindrical spring cover and a round or lozenge-shaped centre plate with a short bow above and a flat leg below, both of which are reeded like the Langton Down Brooch.

Imported Roman brooch with a wide, highly arched flat backed bow, one end rolled forward onto the head to form a hinge for the the pin. Named for the head inscription assumed to be a maker's name. Dated to first half of 1st entury AD.

A first century AD one-piece bow brooch, one end of which is drawn out to form a spring and pin, characterized by its long, narrow, tapering bow, a small crossbar in front of the spring and a forward hook which holds the spring chord.
A Roman brooch dating from approximately 50 to 150 AD. Derived from the earlier Aesica Brooch, and fitted with a hinged pin, the body widens to a triangular plate below the bow.

A Roman Bow Brooch, dating from the later 1st and 2nd centuries often worn in pairs with a linking chain. Characterized by its distinctive head, sometimes enamelled, and resembling the mouth of a trumpet.
A simple, one-piece Bow Brooch with a spring of four coils, sometimes less, its chord tucked beneath its head and variations to bow and catchplate. Dating from the first century AD.

1st or 2nd century Bow Brooch derived from the Colchester. It typically has a pin hinged on a bar held in a cylindrical crossbar, the bow tapering and commonly decorated. Developed examples often have a headloop and enamelled panels.
A mid-1st century AD brooch influenced by the Thistle type, commonly with projections on the upper bow and widening to a triangular plate, often described as a fantail, below.
A Roman Bow Brooch dating from the mid-second to mid-third centuries AD, characterized by the pronounced curvature of the bow, resembling a knee. Usually used by military personnel.

Bow Brooch derived from the Colchester type in the mid to late 1st and 2nd centuries AD. Typically the spring is held in a semi-cylindrical crossbar with closed ends and holding the axial bar and a hook or lug on the head to hold the chord.

Roman, mid to late 1st century, hinged Bow Brooch, related to the Aucissa Brooch, with side knobs on the upper bow, the curvature of which is confined to the brooch's top half, the lower half being straight. Frequently tinned to give a silver appearance.
A two-piece Bow Brooch, derived from the Colchester Brooch, dating from the later first and second centuries AD and so called due to the decorative stud on the upper bow.

Roman bow brooch, dating from the third to fifth centuries AD, with a P-shaped profile and a characteristic crossbar, often adorned with knobs at its end and on the head. Shown in contemporary illustrations as being worn by high ranking males.
The remains of the fine powdery substance produced by male elements of flowering plants, uniquely identifiable to the species it came from.
Use for the remains of any part of a plant.
Use for large remains such as fruit, seeds, plant parts, buds etc.
Use for microscropic remains.

Animal, vegetable and mineral remains including edible materials, drink or residues and unworked imported objects which have been retrieved from archaeological contexts. Also soil samples taken for environmental analysis.
A heavy chain, usually of gold, worn as insignia of office or a mark of fealty or other association from the Middle Ages onwards.
A chain, band or cord, often bearing beads, pearls, jewels etc worn around the neck.
An object, often including gems and ornamentation, used as an adornment, usually on a person.
A bracelet holding charms or being itself a charm.
An object carried about the person for religious purposes.
A stone used for divination.
An object used within a divinational process.
A bowl used for divination.
A tablet used for divination.
Objects used or associated with religious, ritual or funerary activities.
A staff used in divination.
A board used in divination.
A piece of bone used for divination.
A tray used in divination.
A box used for divination.
A figurative representation used in divination.
A die used in divination.
A long shafted weapon with a head that can be edged and/or pointed.
A weapon which is a blade fixed to a long handle.
A tool with a curved blade and a short handle. The blade is on both sides of a line extended from the handle. If just on one side use reaping hook.
An object used in the harvesting or mowing of crops etc.
A long spear designed to be thrown in order to injure or kill a given target.
A long spear with a double barb where one barb is longer than the other.
A long shafted weapon with a pointed head used for thrusting or as a missile.
An object to sit upon. Use narrower terms where known.
A long seat usually without a back.
A strap or series of straps and or bars worn over the mouth of an animal to prevent it biting.
Objects associated with the care and use of animals.
Object used in shaving.
A bowl specifically used for shaving.
A child's bed, usually with rockers.
Movable, usually functional, articles in a room. Use only for objects believed to be furniture where more specific term is not identifiable.
A hollow or rectangular block which acts as a flue allowing the passage of gases or heat.
A slab which can be decorated, shaped, hollow etc. Used for a variety of purposes in construction.
A container in which the Sacrament is carried.
A long handled shallow pan used for frying.
A broad container, usually used for cooking.
A form of frying pan, usually medieval.
Containers used for cooking.
A flat, circular plate, usually made of iron, on which food items are baked.
A container for cooking.
A pan for cooking fish.
A large pot used for boiling.
A vessel with a lid, handle and spout used particularly for boiling water.
Objects primarily intended to be used as a medium of exchange, eg coins.
Official currency printed or written onto paper which can be directly exchanged for goods, services or coins.
An instrument in which air is made to vibrate by the player vibrating their lips and blowing into a mouthpiece.
A trumpet type of instrument made from or in imitation of an animal horn.
A tool for gripping with, often used to pinch objects together.
A set of small pincers used for gripping and plucking.

Tools and equipment with a specific function and those for which a function cannot be assigned with any degree of certainty. Where the function is known, the term is also included under the relevant Class.
A tool with adjustable jaws for gripping and turning objects.
An appliance for holding an object while work is done on it, usually has a pair of jaws.
A piece of animal bone roughly shaped with grooves in which pins are laid and held whilst being sharpened.
Twin bars riveted together which can open and close allowing it to grip objects, usually to lift and move them.
A gripping tool consisting of two hinged arms, usually with serrated jaws.
Equipment used to grip an object.
An instrument in the form of a pair of pincers used to grip and pull objects, usually used in surgery.
A tool used to provide grip and mechanical advantage in applying torque to tighten or loosen objects such as nuts and bolts.
A tool for extracting nails, tacks, pins etc from objects.
A cutting blade with handle used for food preparation and consumption.
A knife that is used for circumcision.
A piece of material worn around the neck. Where it is a collar for an animal use animal collar.
Parts of a building's fabric, structures and associated services, eg drain pipe. If the structure is complete use a monument type thesaurus.
A support for an arch, lintel, beam etc.
A rectangular pier projecting slightly from the face of the wall it buttresses.
A container for the storage and/or transport of food and/or liquid.
A metal container for liquids and foodstuffs, often sealed.
A flexible container, usually leather, for the storage and transportation of wine.
A container used to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.

A flask holding gunpowder used to prime a firearm, ie to provide a small quantity of gunpowder to be put in the pan which would be lit to set off the main charge in the barrel. The powder would often be a finer grade than that used for the charge.
A holder for weapons, explosives and ammunition.
The seal of a papal document
A piece of wax, lead, paper etc, with a stamped design, attached to a document as a guarantee of authenticity.
A lead seal marking newly woven cloth which served as a quality control method in medieval and post medieval periods.
Objects denoting membership and/or authority. Including objects associated with ceremonial rites or symbolising secular power.
A form of armour made up of overlapping squares of protective material.
Use for the construction style of the armour, eg plate armour.
A large medal.
An object originally created to communicate a particular personal belief, achievement, status or membership. It includes articles of adornment or clothing worn primarily for their symbolism.
A grave or sepulchre including a monument.
A stone coffin.
An urn containing a cremation burial.
A container within which a body is placed for burial, usually made of lead or wood.
An object placed to mark the site of a grave.
A part of a coffin that is removable eg handle, mount etc.
A stone placed across a grave.
A stone used to cover a grave.
An object used in the preparation of a body following death. It was used to plug the orifices of the body.
A container for holy relics.
An object related to deaths, burials or funerals.
Movable stand for carrying a body to its grave.
An object that contains bones. Use a monuments thesaurus where this is a site type.
A sheet or cloth used to lay the corpse out in for burial.
A sculpture on top of a tomb etc.
An Egyptian container for holding the entrails of a mummy.
A vessel for keeping the ashes of the dead after cremation.
A container with a human face appliqued onto it, often used as a funerary vessel.
A cast of a person's face taken after death.
Chests connected with the burial of a body.
A stone placed over or at the head or foot of a grave, or at the entrance to a tomb.
A mowing implement with a large curved blade and a long handle.
A tool used to cut or split wood.
Scissor like tool, usually larger than scissors, used for cutting, pruning or trimming.
A cutting tool where the plane of the blade is at right angles to the shaft.
A small cutting hook on a long handle.
A tool with a curved blade and a short handle. The blade is on both sides of a line extended from the handle. If just on one side use reaping hook.
Equipment used primarily to cut, slice, chop etc, using an implement with one or more sharp edges.
A tool with an edged head and a wooden shaft, used for a variety of purposes. Where the function is known use the more specific terms of AXE (TOOL) or AXE (WEAPON).
A small pair of shears with crossed blades, usually used for pruning.
A tool with a handle and curved blade designed to cut through grass sods.
A unifacially worked early stone tool.
A tool with a sharp, concave edge for shaping wood, stone or metal.
A tool used in making wooden hurdles.
Twin blades each with a sharpened edge connected together by a pivot which cut when opened and closed on an object.
A tool for splitting and chopping.
A tool with a curved blade used for pruning.
The cutting or chopping part of an axe; bifacially worked, longitudinal section symmetrical, cutting edge parallel to the direction of the hafted tool as swung.
A tool for cutting substances eg wood, metal etc. Its blade has teeth down one edge.
A shafthole adze with additional hammer knob, normally polished stone.
A hooked blade used to cut crops. All the blade is to one side of the handle rather than being on both sides as in a sickle.
A struck piece of flint or other stone where the length is at least twice the width.
Scissor like object used for cutting, clipping, shearing etc.
The cutting or chopping part of an adze; bifacially worked, longitudinal section symmetrical, cutting edge parallel to the direction of the hafted tool as swung.
A small stone blade with one edge blunted.
A cutting tool for shaping and carving materials, often used in conjunction with a hammer or mallet.
A flake of stone struck from the core where the length is less than twice the width.
A heavy bladed knife.
A tool of flint or other stone, usually worked bifacially, and thought to be a fairly general purpose tool.
A sharp blade used for shaving.
A vessel for the storage and transport of wine.
A container, typically cylindrical with a narrow neck, usually for containing liquids.

Single celled marine organisms. Some form calcareous tests (shells). They are used archaeologically to study the nature of marine environments or establish the presence of marine conditions or products (e.g. imported seaweed).
A bowl adapted by the addition of one or more handles. The handles may be vertical or horizontal loops, or straight, or may comprise vertical or horizontal lugs. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl adapted by the addition of three or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
The arrangement of straps used to control an animal and/or to attach the animal to a vehicle.
Pieces of leather connected to a bridle designed to direct a horse's vision straight forward only.

A jar with a profile that flares outwards from base to rim, but usually with a constriction at the neck. Squat examples, with a height less than the minimum diameter, could be classified as bowls. (Source: MPRG)
A wide mouthed container, usually cylindrical without handles.
A jar with roughly vertical body walls which in some cases may be slightly convex. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with a profile that slopes inwards from base to rim. Particularly shallow examples have been term Peat Pots; this is a term with a restricted distribution in northern England. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with one or more lips added on the outside of the vessel wall over a hole pierced through the wall. The lips do not usually rise above rim level. (Source: MPRG)

A distinctive form of jar being a flat-based, straight-sided or slightly rounded jar with two opposed, upright, rounded, tongue-shaped lugs raised from above the rim and bridged by added, internal struts used to suspend the pot over a fire. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with a rounded profile merging into a rounded base with no discernible basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A jar adapted by the addition of one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)

A jar adapted by the addition of a pierced bunghole just above the base. The hole can be simply a hole in the wall of the vessel or made through a circular knob or disc of clay. There may be one or two vertical loop handles. (Source: MPRG)
A jar whose profile resembles two truncated cones joined together at the widest point, forming a carination at the centre of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with a roughly cylindrical or slightly flaring lower body and a rounded, inward sloping shoulder. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with an evenly rounded profile. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with vertical concave body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A small hemispherical or flared bowl with a solid pointed base or spike. May include a wick lip at the rim and one or two pierced lugs for suspension. (Source: MPRG)
An appliance that produces light by means of a liquid fuel and a wick.
A mug with more-or-less straight side which flare out from the rim to the wider base. (Source: MPRG)
A drinking vessel with a handle.
A German stoneware form of conical mug. (Source: MPRG)
A decorative container for serving cream.
A container used to serve food from.
A brush or perforated ball on a handle used to sprinkle holy water.
A weapon featuring a single or double edged head attached to a wooden shaft.
An axe designed for fighting could be single or double headed and used with one or two hands. Where use is thought to be ceremonial multiple index with ceremonial object.
A layer of impervious material, such as lead or bitumen, usually placed between a wall and a roofline to exclude water.Impervious material to exclude water.
Fragments of a structure, usually material that has been worked.
A rope, clog or fetter used to limit the movement of animals through the tying of two or more feet together.
A bead or group of beads used in prayer.
A flake or blade tool with more than one notch on its edge.
A scraper with retouch at right angles to the bulb of percussion.
A small flake or blade with a series of notches along the edge.
A wide-mouthed, narrow based flared bowl associated with dairying activities. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl with straight sides which turn outwards, forming an obtuse angle with the base, i.e. and inverted, truncated, conical shape. (Source: MPRG)
A round container open at the top, usually to hold liquids.
A flared bowl of medium to deep depth, with wide horizontal everted rim. These vessels may have a single handle. (Source: MPRG)

A device used to slow the fall of a person or an object from a great height. When open it consists of a large umberella like sheet with wires or ropes converging from the edge to a harness attached to the lower end.
A musical instrument producing sound by the use of strings.
A musical instrument producing sound by air vibrating.
Use more specific term where available.
A musical instrument producing sound from its own body.
Musical instruments by how the sound is produced by the instrument.
A musical instrument producing sound from a membrane.
An item used in caring for the body for appearance and cleanliness.
A tool for cleaning a person's nails.
A set of clippers for finger or toe nails.
A small file for manicuring a person's nails with.
An associated group of manicure implements.
An item used as part of a manicure.
A small, usually hinged, box made of copper alloy which protected a seal.
An object worn on the head.
A band of cloth or other material worn around the head, not totally covering it.
A form of tumbler lock key which pushes the bolt.
A device for operating a lock.
A net worn over the hair to hold it in place.
A container which holds a set quantity of a substance, can be graduated to show quantities.
A container holding a set quantity used specifically for measuring grains or cereal.
A shallow dish or plate used to serve main meals off or to serve sweetmeats, these usually being painted with devices and riddles.
A single or pair of figures or plates attached to a joist or bar either at the time of construction or later and built into the wall to provide support.
A long narrow two edged sword with a guarded hilt, used as a thrusting weapon.
A flat heavy slightly curved blade.
A broad design of sword used for cutting and slashing.

A cutting and thrusting weapon consisting of a blade and a hilt. The blade varies in length and width and the cutting edge can vary from being purely the point to being one or both sides or a combination of these.
A curved sword designed to cut with used by cavalry.
A two edged broadsword.
A fencing sword often used for recreational purposes.
A single edged sword.
A type of sword often used by infantry.
A weapon with a sharp edge or point.
A container from which liquid is drunk.
A drinking vessel, made of glass, designed expressly to be used for the consumption of wine.
A straight sided drinking vessel, usually with a wide mouth and without handles.
A beaker adapted by having a pedestal base. The upper part of the pedestal is solid and may be thrown as an integral part of the vessel, or added as a separate element.(Source: MPRG)
A beaker with a construction above the base to form a waisted profile. (Source: MPRG)
A container from which liquid is drunk.
A beaker with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A long slim candle used for igniting objects.
An object used in lighting a fire, lamp, oven, candle etc.
An object which has been carved. Use more specific term where known.
A projectile or group of projectiles fired from a weapon, usually solid rather than explosive.
A number of small iron balls, each about the size of a cricket ball, often numbering nine, wired together and bound in a canvas bag.
A type of jug decorated with applied figures in the form of knights in armour. (Source: MPRG)
A jug form characterized by the addition of three applied feet. The feet are usually short and stubby. Double index with the appropriate profile type. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a constriction between the upper and lower body sections. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with little or no discernible neck and an even, convex profile form rim to base, which are roughly equal in diameter. (Source: MPRG)

A type of stoneware jug dating to the 16th and 17th centuries, popularly known as a Bellarmine jug, shaped in the figure of a bearded man. They were produced in the Cologne region, particularly Frechen.
A jug with more or less straight sides that flare out from a narrow rim or neck to a wider base. (Source: MPRG)
Any type of jug with decoration representing the form of a human face or figure. The shape of the vessel often forms the outline of the human face or figure. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a maximum diameter in the lower half of the vessel and with no discernible shoulder. (Source: MPRG)
A container used to serve drinks and liquids.

A jug that incorporates a practical joke or conundrum into its construction. A combination of cut-outs in the body and hollow handles and tubes render conventional use impossible. The puzzle being to work out how to empty it. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a sinuous profile where the rim, girth and base are of roughly equal diameter, and the neck and lower body are narrower.(Source: MPRG)

A jug with a pierced bunghole just above the base. the bunghole can be made through an applied knob or disc of clay, or simply a hole in the vessel wall. In use, the hole would probably have had a tap or spigot attached. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a body profile resembling two cones joined together at their widest points, producing a distinct carination. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a rounded profile and a rounded base, i.e. with no discernible basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A container for holding or pouring liquids, usually having a handle or lip.

A jug form which usually has a short, narrow neck and rim and an applied spout. The spout is usually tubular and free standing, or attached to the rim/neck, rising from just above the high shoulder or girth. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a roughly cylindrical or slightly flaring lower body, a pronounced inward-sloping shoulder and a marked neck/body angle. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with an evenly rounded profile and a discernible basal angle; the maximum diameter is near the mid point and in the upper half of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with straight vertical sides and roughly equal rim and base diameters. (Source: MPRG)
Objects relating to the punishment and restraint of humans.
A chain used to inhibit a slave's freedom.
A cylindrical container, usually bulging outward in the middle and usually held together by metal hoops.
A tool used in the separation of fibres from the bark in flax working.

A curfew consisting of a half-bell shape with a raised flange around the cut-away side. A vertical loop handle joins the flange and body. The form is adapted for use against a wall and some are highly decorated. (Source: MPRG)
A metal or ceramic cover for an open fire or hearth.
A metal bar to strengthen the heel and or sole of a piece of footwear.
Items which are part of or added to footwear.
A belt worn around the waist from which a sword is hung. Where worn over the shoulder use baldric.
A container for a pyx which is often a canopied niche in a wall.
Use for structural objects within or connected to a place of worship, eg font. For structures, use a monuments thesaurus.
A vessel comprising two or more dishes, generally of small size, joined together. There may be one or more handles of varying forms. (Source: MPRG)
A container used for holding or serving food, especially an open shallow container.

A jar adapted by the addition of three or more vertical loop handles. They may be equally spaced, or confined to one section of the circumference. Profile vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
A thin slab of hard material, such as baked clay, shaped and used in quantity as a roofing material.

Plain clay tile suspended, loose, from the top edge of a tiling lath or batten so as to overlap two courses below. It is held in place by a small wooden peg wedged into, or passed through, one (or sometimes both) of two holes in the head of the tile.
A container for drinking, with a base and stem but without handles.
A container used with an alembic as part of the distilling process.
Containers used for industrial or scientific purposes.
An article laid over the floor or ground as a protcetive and or decorative covering.
Thick material, usually woven, used as a covering for the floor.
A mat used on the floor as a covering.
Soft coverings and associated objects used within buildings.
Decorative ornamentation worn in the hair.
An object originally created to be worn on the human body or on clothing for ornamentation rather than for protection or as a body covering.
A ring used to hold hair in place.
A comb or slide used to hold hair in place.
A pin used to hold hair in place.
A collective name for the pans and fire bars used in the making of salt through evaporation.
A secondary or incidental product of a process.
A type of dagger, usually used by civilians in the medieval period, with a H shaped hilt.
A short stabbing weapon with a pointed blade. In Medieval period daggers served a dual function being also used as knives.
A small spoon used with incense.
The weight attached to a spindle used for handspinning.
Equipment used in the production of textiles.
A jug with little or no discernible neck and an even, convex profile form rim to base, which are roughly equal in diameter. (Source: MPRG)
Any type of jug with decoration representing the form of a human face or figure. The shape of the vessel often forms the outline of the human face or figure. (Source: MPRG)

A jug form which usually has a short, narrow neck and rim and an applied spout. The spout is usually tubular and free standing, or attached to the rim/neck, rising from just above the high shoulder or girth. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a constriction between the upper and lower body sections. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a body profile resembling two cones joined together at their widest points, producing a distinct carination. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with an evenly rounded profile and a discernible basal angle; the maximum diameter is near the mid point and in the upper half of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a rounded profile and a rounded base, i.e. with no discernible basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a roughly cylindrical or slightly flaring lower body, a pronounced inward-sloping shoulder and a marked neck/body angle. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with a maximum diameter in the lower half of the vessel and with no discernible shoulder. (Source: MPRG)

A type of stoneware jug dating to the 16th and 17th centuries, popularly known as a Bellarmine jug, shaped in the figure of a bearded man. They were produced in the Cologne region, particularly Frechen.
A jug form characterized by the addition of three applied feet. The feet are usually short and stubby. Double index with the appropriate profile type. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with more or less straight sides that flare out from a narrow rim or neck to a wider base. (Source: MPRG)
A type of jug decorated with applied figures in the form of knights in armour. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with straight vertical sides and roughly equal rim and base diameters. (Source: MPRG)
A container for holding or pouring liquids, usually having a handle or lip.
A jug with a sinuous profile where the rim, girth and base are of roughly equal diameter, and the neck and lower body are narrower.(Source: MPRG)

A jug that incorporates a practical joke or conundrum into its construction. A combination of cut-outs in the body and hollow handles and tubes render conventional use impossible. The puzzle being to work out how to empty it. (Source: MPRG)

A jug with a pierced bunghole just above the base. the bunghole can be made through an applied knob or disc of clay, or simply a hole in the vessel wall. In use, the hole would probably have had a tap or spigot attached. (Source: MPRG)

A vessel composed of three or more equally spaced lobes and usually, a waisted or pedestal base. The handles are vertical loops, generally of strap form. There may be two opposed handles, or one per lobe junction. (Source: MPRG)
A cup adapted by the addition of a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A cup with a marked change of angle in the body wall, generally at the point of maximum girth. (Source: MPRG)
A small open container, usually with one handle, used for drinking.
A cup with sides falring outwards from the base towards the rim, usually with a single handle.

A form of cup which incorporates a joke or conundrum, where cut-outs or holes in the body make conventional use impossible. The puzzle is how to drink from it without getting wet; usually achieved by sucking through a hollow rim or handle. (Source: MPRG)
A cup with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A cup with a rounded body beneath a deep neck. There may be a marked junction between neck and body and the angle of the neck may be inward-sloping, upright or everted. (Source: MPRG)
A vessel composed of three or more separate compartments, joined together and pierced to allow liquid to flow from one to another. There may be one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)

A piece of metal or wood, or other material used in the manufacture of an item, intended for further fabrication and therefore representing an unfinished object. Usually relates to objects manufactured in quantity such as coins, knives etc.
An object that is to become a padlock key. It has the correct shape but has yet to be cut to fit the lock.
An object which is going to become a key. It has the shape but has not been cut to fit a lock.
A light plough used to break up the earth. It does not have a mouldboard to turn the earth.
A covering for the hand.
A type of comb for flax or hemp.
A clasp on a cope.
An item used to join together clothing (includes footwear).
A measuring container to measure dry goods.
A container which holds a set quantity of a substance, can be graduated to show quantities.
Use for the remains of any part of a human.
Remains of any of the groups of animals characterized by having a bony or cartilaginous spinal column (backbone) and a well developed brain.

A shallow ceramic or metal libation bowl, usually with no handles or feet. The Greeks called it a phiale, the Romans a patera. Used in religious practice. Could contain mixed wine and water but also unmixed wine, honey, oil, water, or milk.
A container used to hold and or pour out liquid in honour of a deity.
A container used to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.
A container used to serve food from.
A small dish, often with a handle, for soup, porridge etc.
A small shield.
A type of shield, usually wooden and circular.
A small round shield.
A protective piece of armour carried in the hand to protect the bearer from weapons.
Protective covering for the body.
A curfew consisting of a half section of an inverted bowl. The form has been adapted for use against a wall. (Source: MPRG)

Amoebae which are partially enclosed in external shells, or tests. Found in wet soils, freshwater, decaying organic matter and on vegetation such as mosses. Used to examine environmental factors such as water acidity and moisture content.
A projection on which an object is fastened or which can pass through eyelets to fasten.
A comb used to comb fibre before spinning.
A precious or semi precious stone used within jewellery.
Part of a piece of jewellery.
Includes equipment for the consumption of food.
A pin used to hold meat together whilst cooking.
Each of a pair of long narrow pieces of wood, usually pointed and turned up at the front, fastened under the feet for travel on snow.
Objects used or associated with the movement of goods or people.
A brush used to apply soap to the body when shaving.
An axe designed for fighting could be single or double headed and used with one or two hands. Where use is thought to be ceremonial multiple index with ceremonial object.
A long shaft topped by an axe head. Differs from a battleaxe by the length of the shaft.
An axe designed and weighted to be thrown.
A weapon featuring a single or double edged head attached to a wooden shaft.

A cylindrical bomb, designed by Barnes Wallis in 1941, containing hydrostatic pistols measuring water pressure for detonation at a depth of 30 feet. Dropped whilst fast revolving at a height of 60 feet at 240-250 mph to maximise the bouncing effect.
A weapon which causes damage through exploding.
A hollow case containg explosive, incendiary, smoke-producing, poisonous or other offensive material, designed to be deposited, dropped, thrown or shot froma mortar.
A stone basin for containing holy water often set in inside walls of a church, especially near the entrance.
A self-propelled wheeled vehicle that does not operate on rails, such as trains or trolleys.
A human-powered, pedal-driven, single-track vehicle, having two wheels attached to a frame, one behind the other.
A machine that can be flown in the air, now specifically an aeroplane or helicopter.
Any vehicle for transportation on land.
A means of transportation.
Any vehicle/craft used for transportation on water.
The constituent parts of a kiln.
Objects concerned with marriage and weddings.
Head wear of a bride.
Used to detach flakes from cores or tools.
A non perforated stone used as a hammer in making stone tools.
Equipment used in the making of tools.

A specific form of rounded jug with a wide mouth, a large parrot-beak spout and three handles in the form of wide straps that spring from the rim to the girth.The spout may have a lug applied to the outside. (Source: MPRG)
Equipment used to scrape and smooth objects.
Equipment for turning wood, metal, bone etc.
An object worn, or carried, to cover the face.
An object, usually wood or metal used to force open or keep open another object. Often used to split timber by striking the thick end and forcing the wood apart.
Equipment used to bore a hole in a solid object through the use of a twisting, drilling action.
The cutting part of an auger.
A cross with the image of Christ upon it.
A cross designed to be placed upon an altar.
A large cross designed to be carried in a procession.
A cross connected to a reliquary or containing a relic itself.
An object consisting of a vertical member with a transverse member. Usually there is a religious or ritual significance to such an object.
A jug with a constriction between the upper and lower body sections. (Source: MPRG)
An instrument which has a cupped mouthpiece and may have a straight, bent or curved tube. It includes slide trumpets like the trombone.
A musket fired by a piece of burning match being held in the lock which, when the trigger was pulled, would contact with powder in the pan to set off the charge.

A musket where a wheel on the lock is wound back and when the trigger is pulled the wheel moves dragging pieces of iron pyrites over the lock tocreate sparks and set powder in the pan off and so firing the main charge.
A heavy, smooth bore, long barrelled weapon, fired from the shoulder.
A musket with a snaphance lock. This lock consisted of a flint and steel but the pan cover was separate to the steel unlike the flintlock.

A musket fired by use of a flintlock. A gunflint is held in the lock which on pulling the trigger pushes back the pan cover creating sparks which set off the powder in the pan causing a flame to go through and set off the charge in the barrel.
A hand held long barrelled gun.
Items which are used in clothing but which are not necessarily a separate item of clothing.
Portable device that generate stable and consistent flame by combining flammable liquids or pressurised gases with a spark.

A small, metal, crescent-shaped object, often highly decorated, worn across the upper part of the chest by military personnel as a badge of rank and as a symbolic representation of the former piece of armour (see GORGET (ARMOUR).
Clothes, items of attire and ornament and objects for personal use by individuals, including toiletries.
A container, usually glass, used for storing medicinal substances.
A container used to hold, store, examine or create medicinal substances such as ointments, medicines, drugs, pills, tablets etc.
Cube-shaped shot.
A container filled with musket balls, metal fragments, nails etc. Designed to kill or maim the enemy at a close range.
An image of a deity used as a focus for worship, usually used for non Christian images.
A jar used to store medicines, drugs or the constituent parts of medicines and drugs.
A container used to hold, store, examine or create medicinal substances such as ointments, medicines, drugs, pills, tablets etc.
A prepared core from which a single flake or blade has been produced. The technique was primarily used in the Palaeolithic and Neolithic.
The piece of stone or flint remaining after flakes have been produced. This is a by-product of tool manufacture.
An instrument of torture used to compress one or both thumbs.
A vessel to contain the wafers used in the Eucharist.
A container to receive milk during the milking process.
A paddle blade, typically of wood, from a horizontal or vertical mill wheel.
Equipment for the hunting and capture of animals.
A staff with a bent head used by a shepherd. For a crook used by a bishop use crozier.
A receptacle used as a home for bees, traditionally made of thick straw work in the shape of a dome,but sometimes made of wood.
Equipment used in the preparation and maintenance of ground, and in the planting and maintenance of crops.
Objects associated with cultivation, the rearing of livestock, gathering, hunting, fishing, horticulture and the primary processing of foodstuffs.
A long handled fork with two or three long curved tines for tossing straw or hay.
A moveable section of light fence, traditionally made from wattle and used for herding livestock, to separate land or to act as river bank revetments.
A portable beehive, often made of straw.
Objects used to thresh and winnow crops.
A flexible holder for a bladed tool or weapon.
A dish comprising three or more lobes, usually equally spaced. There may be one or more animal or other figures inside the base and two or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A container used for holding or serving food, especially an open shallow container.
A round, oval or rectangular straight-sided dish, divided into two or more units or compartments of varying sizes by vertical slab partitions. (Source: MPRG)

A type of dish specifically designed to catch the juices from roasting meat. The dish may be oval, semi-circular, rectangular or wedge-shaped. Usually has one or two handles located on one long side and a lip at one or both short sides. (Source: MPRG)
A dish with straight or convex sides that turn inwards, forming an acute basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A dish adapted by the addition of a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A vessel comprising two or more dishes, generally of small size, joined together. There may be one or more handles of varying forms. (Source: MPRG)

A wide, shallow, straight-sided, circular DISH, with concentric divisions, formed by vertical partitions, or by concentric grooving around the base. The use is uncertain but the name implies it was designed to hold feed for domestic fowl. (Source: MPRG)
A vehicle used in warfare, racing or to carry passengers.
Any form of fastener used to fasten a girdle together.
Use for the function that the armour is intended for, eg parades, field use, decorative etc.
Armour used to protect a horse.
The front piece of horse armour.
A long narrow spoon used to remove cosmetics or medicines from narrow necked containers.
an instrument or tool used for medical purposes.
An end scraper made on a flake that is wider than it is long. (Source: SARC)
An end scraper that has a retouched end which is convex in form. (Source: SARC)
A flint blade or flake with retouch on one or both ends.
An end scraper which has a retouched end that is concave in form. (Source: SARC)

An end scraper which has a retouched end that is straight in form. If the tool blank is a blade or flake, and the retouch is abrupt retouch rather than scraper retouch then use either Truncated blade or Truncated flake. (Source: SARC)
A flake or blade with retouch along one or more edges.
An object used to weigh something down or to measure the weight of another object.
One of a set used in pharmacy to measure volume or weight for dispensing.
A jar adapted by the addition of one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A jar adapted by the addition of a single handle. Profiles vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
A stick or rod with an attached lash.
An object used to provide heat for various purposes eg to cook or to heat a room etc.
A fan designed to fan the coals of a fire to increase the oxygen around the coals and thus to help the fire burn.
A dish in which an offering is made.
Dress designed to be worn for theatrical purposes.
Clothing worn specifically for formalised dancing.
A style of dressing including all the clothes, accessories etc worn at one time, as in a particular country or period.
A small object designed to hold together the two ends of a cuff.
A dial for showing the hours of the night by the moon.
An instrument used to measure the passing of time.

A jar adapted by the addition of one straight handle, attached to either rim or body, and usually with a lip at right angles to the handle. The body is generally rounded, with a short neck and an everted rim. (Source: MPRG)
A jar adapted by the addition of a straight handle and three feet. Profiles vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
A stringed instrument usually played with a bow.
An musical instrument where the strings are played by plucking or bowing. Use more specific term where known.
A stringed musical instrument, usually played with a bow.
An instrument played mainly by bowing the strings. Use narrower terms where known.
An axe used by the Saxons and Franks for throwing.
A moveable covering, usually to a window, made up of a frame containing slats set at an angle. Allows air to circulate but limits light entering.
Objects used to secure buildings, furniture or objects or used in the finishing and (non-structural) decoration of buildings, equipment and furniture.
A cloth or scarf worn over the head as a covering.
Articles used in charging guns and ordnance.
A metal sphere, usually lead, fired from a musket. Often bound into a cartridge.
A stick for digging up roots etc. Sometimes a stone weight is attached.
Equipment for digging earth, etc.
Remains of wheat (Triticum sp.) grain (caryopyses). These grains are sometimes itemised separately.
The fruit or seed of a cereal plant such as wheat, corn etc.
Remains of barley (Hordeum sp.) grain (caryopyses). These grains are sometimes itemised separately.
An instrument in which strings are vibrated by an internal wheel cranked by a handle.
A band of leather, cloth etc worn usually around the waist to support clothing, carry weapons, pouches, tools etc or as a decoration.
A flared bowl of medium to deep depth, with wide horizontal everted rim. These vessels may have a single handle. (Source: MPRG)
A wide-mouthed, narrow based flared bowl associated with dairying activities. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl with straight sides which turn outwards, forming an obtuse angle with the base, i.e. and inverted, truncated, conical shape. (Source: MPRG)
A round container open at the top, usually to hold liquids.
A shallow channel to collect rain water, attached to the eaves of a building.
A horn containing gun powder used for priming a firearm.
A portable writing-case or surface, especially one for use on the lap. May contain compartments for storing writing materials and paper.
A small knife used in surgery.
A box used for storage or shipping.
This does not include food or liquid storage.
A container for holding votive offerings.
An object for making an offering to a deity. It includes objects concerned with curses, sacrifices, and prayers and their related objects.
Part of a musical instrument.
The part of an organ or similar instrument into which air is pushed then is emitted through the pipes.
A tool with an edged head and a wooden shaft, used for a variety of purposes.
A small, handled axe.
A tool with an edged head and a wooden shaft, used for a variety of purposes. Where the function is known use the more specific terms of AXE (TOOL) or AXE (WEAPON).
The spanning of an opening without the use of a lintel.
A plate used during circumcision.
Equipment used to perform circumcision.
A round container, open and wide at the top with sides sloping inwards toward the bottom.

Any object, often made of ceramic or 'porcelain' used for the purposes of personal cleansing, especially fittings such as toilets, baths and basins. May survive as fragments in the archaeological record. Use more specific term where known.

A flask holding gunpowder used to prime a firearm, ie to provide a small quantity of gunpowder to be put in the pan which would be lit to set off the main charge in the barrel. The powder would often be a finer grade than that used for the charge.

A device used to slow the fall of a person or an object from a great height. When open it consists of a large umberella like sheet with wires or ropes converging from the edge to a harness attached to the lower end.
Use for items of personal equipment used in a military context, eg AMMUNITION POUCH, PRIMING FLASK.
A container used to hold ammunition.
An object used in the transmission of the written word or drawn image.
A thin vegetable based sheet used for writing, drawing, printing etc.
A device to extract juice, usually from fruit.
An ornamental wand or rod carried as a symbol of authority
An encircling ornament for the head, usually denoting sovereignty.
A staff used for ceremonial purposes, often highly decorated.
A seat of state, often highly decorative, used by monarchs, bishops, etc.
A band worn around the head to denote sovereignty.
A symbol with secular signifigance, particularly implying authority.
A device for removing the core from fruit.
A tool for removing the core from an apple.
A mug with a rounded body profile below a deep neck. There may be a marked junction between the neck and the body. The neck may be inward-sloping, upright or everted. (Source: MPRG)
A drinking vessel with a handle.
A specific German Stoneware form of rounded mug. (Source: MPRG)
The metal ornamental bedding and surround in which a gem or stone is mounted.
Objects used for looking after hair.
A comb made up of riveting together sections between connecting plates.
A toothed strip of material used to disentangle hair. Can be either single or double sided.
Equipment used on a bird of prey for hawking.
A small spherical bell for fastening onto the leg of a hawk.
A pattern used to create a consistent shape for an object, often hollow and reusable.
A mould in which bullets or musket balls are cast.
A comparatively thick, roughly shaped piece of stone, usually square or rectangular, used to roof structures.
A triangular sectioned rod probably used for making sparks.
A stone or stone platform in a fire upon which cooking vessels are placed.
An object used in the lighting of an area.
An object used to stop a candle burning.
Urn with a heavy rim or collar which may be vertical or slightly inverted. This may carry incised or impressed decoration, particularly herring-bone, encircling lines, hurdling or chevron decoration.
A vase-like receptacle, especially a large bulbous one with a foot.
A large container with lip and handle usually for milk.
A vessel used to produce butter from milk through agitation.
A medical container for collecting urine for study.
A small cutting hook on a long handle.
Two or more tablets of wood attached by a string or leather thong, with a raised border one side of which was filled with wax to write upon. Thin wooden tablets with ink writing are also known.

A small notebook formed from two tablets usually made of wood, bone, ivory or metal. They were given as commemorative gift by a consul on his installation and often decorated with the image of the consul.
A deliberately made missile fired from a sling.
A missile fired from a sling.
The remains of the shell of an oyster.
Use for the remains of any part of a mollusc. Use more specific term if known.
Molluscs found in salt or brackish water. Use more specific term where known.
The remains of a shell of a mussel.
A pole with a blade used for rowing or steering a boat by leverage against the water.
A heavy weight used to moor a ship.
A maritime fitting for spacing rigging elements and preventing entanglement.
A long handled hook used for pulling a boat to a place eg another boat, or pushing off from a place.
A short, broad bladed oar used without a rowlock.
A device on a boat's gunwale serving as a fulcrum for an oar and keeping it in place.
Equipment used on or with water craft.
A circular toy which spins on a point.
A circular toy which spins on a point. Can be kept spinning by whipping with a lash.
A hard, unglazed earthenware, characteristically reddish-brown in colour, used in decoration and construction.
A construction material made by the firing or baking of clay.
A metal spike for fixing one object to another.
A nail used for roofing.
A nail adapted so that the end of the nail can be turned over and driven back into the substance through which it has passed.
A flat nail of the same thickness that tapers in width to a point.
The forms of armour used to protect various parts of the body.
Armour designed to protect the chest, sometimes permanently attached to a backplate.
A Roman mixing vessel with a gritted inner surface.
Tool used in dentistry either for examination or surgery.
A small hand operated rotary drill used by dentists.
The support from which a lamp was hung.
A large portable container used for carrying items such as clothes, papers etc.
A small bag or pouch for carrying money, especially coins.
A flexible container with an opening at one end.
A small bag or detachable outside pocket.
A container, capable of being carried by hand, often for carrying such items as clothes, keys, money, etc.
An intially worked object, prepared to be made into a final product.
An initially worked quern that is still to be finished.
A purse, sometimes of bronze, usually worn on the forearm.
Kingdom of living things, lacking chlorophyll, that obtain their food from organic matter, usually by absorption. They include mushrooms, moulds and yeasts.
Characteristic waste flakes struck off in the manufacture of adzes.
Waste flakes and cores produced in the manufacture of stone implements.
A strip of lead holding small pieces of glass (quarries) in a window.
A support projecting from a wall.
Weight used for weighing specific quantities and qualities of gold.
The bottom part of a sleeve, can be ornamental.
An instrument which creates a buzzing noise.
A spirally grooved object used to join two or more materials together.

A jar adapted by the addition of a pierced bunghole just above the base. The hole can be simply a hole in the wall of the vessel or made through a circular knob or disc of clay. There may be one or two vertical loop handles. (Source: MPRG)
Military weapon with long shaft and bill style head. For agricultural style bills use bill hook.

A stone core made on a large thick flake and characterized by a distinctive, unworked or slightly-shaped handle section. The platform is plain and microblades are detached mainly form the front section of the core. (Source: SARC)
A tool used to twist fibres together to create yarn.
Container with a handle, spout and narrow neck.
A board or slab, often with a thumb hole in it, used by an artist to hold and mix colours on.
A pan with a perforated bottom for straining or rinsing food.
A long shafted weapon with a pointed head used for thrusting or as a missile.
A long spear used on horseback.
A smooth bore weapon usually for firing shot as opposed to bullets.
A handheld weapon that fires missiles by means of explosives.
A flint tool with a convex top and flat base.
A drinking vessel with two or more handles.
A mirror designed to be held by hand.

A form of cup which incorporates a joke or conundrum, where cut-outs or holes in the body make conventional use impossible. The puzzle is how to drink from it without getting wet; usually achieved by sucking through a hollow rim or handle. (Source: MPRG)
An object which perforated the axle cap, passing through the axle and so keeping the wheel in place. Usually made of metal, though can be made of wood or antler.
An axe used by the Saxons and Franks for throwing.
An axe designed and weighted to be thrown.

A form of bronze axehead of middle or late Bronze Age date in which narrow, high flanges on both faces are hammered over to enclose the axe haft. Some types feature a side loop to further assist hafting.
Parts which make up a maritime vessel.
Any part of a maritime vessel. Use more specific term where known.
A fan used to create an air current to separate grain from chaff.
An instrument for writing or drawing with ink.
A tool for holding or compressing objects together.
A vessel comprising of two or more bowls, generally of small size, joined together. There may be one or more handles of varying forms. (Source: MPRG)
A plate used in the Eucharist.
A covered chalice used for the Eucharist.
A stemmed cup used for holding the wine during communion.
A vessel to contain wine or water used in the Eucharist.
A spoon used to dispense communion wine.
An object used in the Eucharist.
A chamber for heating or baking objects.
A covering for the head with a peak to shade the eyes.
A close fitting cap covering the sides, back and top of the head.
A hand held instrument for producing a current of air for cooling the user. It is both a practical and a fashion item.
A floor composed of small cubes (tesserae) of variously coloured materials (eg tile, stone, marble). In the Roman period, mosaics often depict a religious theme or symbol.
A painting executed directly onto a wall.
Objects forming fragments or parts of a building's fabric, structures and associated services, eg drain pipes. Where an object is a fixture or fitting use the separate class.
A two dimensional image using paint applied to the surface of a ceiling.
A three-cornered arrowhead.
A triangular arrowhead retouched to form a central tang and lateral barbs. The sides may be straight or concave.
A lid resembling an inverted dish with a flat top and an integral or added knob or loop handle. (Source: MPRG)
An instrument whirled to produce sound by a piece of flexible wood hitting against a ratchet wheel.
A pipe in which a substance is smoked.
A clay tube with a small bowl at the end used for smoking tobacco.
A discoidal flint tool often has a ground edge.
A long rope with a noose at one end for catching animals.
A stick used as an aid or accessory when walking.
A type of mace with bolster shaped stone or metal head on a wooden or metal shaft. Where the use is thought to be ceremonial, double index with ceremonial object.
A stone or metal head on a wooden shaft. Where the use is thought to be ceremonial multiple index with ceremonial object.
A handheld weapon that fires missiles by means of explosives.
A gun designed to be held in one hand. It has a short barrel length compared to long guns.
A hand held long barrelled gun.
A smooth bore weapon usually for firing shot as opposed to bullets.
An agricultural implement with sharp blades for cutting or turning over the earth.
A Romano-British brooch type, appearing at the end of the first century AD and developing during the second, characterized by a flat plate to which the pin and catchplate are attached at the back.
A disc-shaped plate brooch.
An enamelled plate brooch, dating from the second and third centuries AD, depicting an animal often with religious significance, such as hares, birds, fish and horses, sometimes with rider.
A knife with a hinged blade which folds into a slot in the handle.
A very small flint or other stone tool.
A microlith retouched to a half moon or crescent shape. (Source: SARC)
A microlith retouched to a rectangular shape. (Source: SARC)
A microlith retouched to a thomboid shape. (Source: SARC)
A microlith retouched to a trapezium shape. (Source: SARC)
A microlith retouched to a triangular shape. They can be sub-divided into scalene and isosceles triangles. (Source: SARC)
A microlith retouched to a point, usually with oblique retouch. They are synonymous with 'obliquely blunted points' of the English Mesolithic. (Source: SARC)
A shallow, usually circular, dish on which food is served or eaten from.
A mug with straight sides flaring outwards from the base towards the rim. (Source: MPRG)
A drinking vessel with a handle.
A long handled scoop usually used with liquids.
A ring worn around the neck.
A circular frame or disc arranged to revolve on an axle and used to facilitate the motion of a vehicle.
An instrument for viewing or measuring information on heavenly bodies.
An instrument for measuring the angular distance of objects, and particularly used for navigation by noting the position of the stars.
Remains of Bryozoa, which are small animals, usually marine, that form colonies producing coral-like skeletons or jelly-like masses.
A small shovel for adding or placing coals onto a fire.
A decorative tile incorporated into the front of a tile stove.
A piece of fabric with a design, used as an emblem for military or naval purposes, signalling, decoration, display, propaganda etc.
A device to catch and hold one object to another, eg a door to a doorpost.
A tool for smoothing objects.
An iron bar or ingot of distinctive form with a pinched end previously thought to have been used as a medium of exchange in the Iron Age but is now believed to be a roughout for a sword.
A cup with a rounded body beneath a deep neck. There may be a marked junction between neck and body and the angle of the neck may be inward-sloping, upright or everted. (Source: MPRG)
A narrow container, especially one in which food or water for animals is put.
A pestle used specifically for grinding cosmetics.
An object used with cosmetics.
An partly-worked millstone that is still to be finished.
A metal pin with two arms which is passed through a hole and held in place by the springing apart of the arms.
A small piece of metal or other material used to hold objects together.

A vessel composed of three or more equally spaced lobes and usually, a waisted or pedestal base. The handles are vertical loops, generally of strap form. There may be two opposed handles, or one per lobe junction. (Source: MPRG)
A small open container, usually with one handle, used for drinking.
A shrine often including representations of a god or gods or other holy relics which could be carried by worshippers whilst travelling.
A small flake or blade with a series of notches along the edge.
A flake or blade tool with more than one notch on its edge.
Use for deposits formed as a result of the modification of soils by humans for agricultural purposes.
A light covering for the foot, usually worn indoors.
A jug with little or no discernible neck and an even, convex profile form rim to base, which are roughly equal in diameter. (Source: MPRG)
An ornament made of thin plates of gold or silver, with repousse ornament.
A supended ornament.

A device used to slow the fall of a person or an object from a great height. When open it consists of a large umberella like sheet with wires or ropes converging from the edge to a harness attached to the lower end.
A calculating table or frame, specifically one in which balls slide upon wires. Used for the mechanical solution of arithmetical problems.
A two post structure with cross beam for hanging criminals.
A bowl with no noticeable base angle, so that the walls and base form a continuous rounded curve (Source: MPRG)
A container made of interwoven strips of pliable materials, such as cane or reed, often carried by a handle.
Any type of jug with decoration representing the form of a human face or figure. The shape of the vessel often forms the outline of the human face or figure. (Source: MPRG)
Equipment used primarily to bring percussive force onto an object.
A tool that produces a mark on an object through the application of percussive force through the stamp.
This includes cosmetic containers.
A bowl used to wash in.
A container with a perforated bottom through which material is shaken or poured.
A spike for separating the strands of a rope.
Equipment used to pierce or puncture other objects through the use of a point.
A holder for a candle, usually fitted to another object.
A piece of wood attached to a thong that makes a roaring sound when the thong is whirled.
A figure, representation or image, which is itself regarded as sacred.
The peg of a stringed instrument which, when tightened or loosened, tunes the string of the instrument.
A carved head of Iron Age or Roman date, with uncertain use.
A three dimensional representation usually done in wood, ivory or stone.
A rail used to suspend objects from.
A drinking vessel with two or more handles.
A vessel with a specially adapted mouthpiece, to assist in feeding young animals and humans.
A tent especially one made of bark, rushes or skins spread over a set of arched poles that are lashed together.
A hand held device for mashing food, usually with a flat holed base attached to a handle.
A bowl with a single loop handle crossing over the top of the vessel from one side of the rim to the other. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl adapted by the addition of one or more handles. The handles may be vertical or horizontal loops, or straight, or may comprise vertical or horizontal lugs. (Source: MPRG)
An ornamental chain, worn around the ankle.
A mug with roughly vertical body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A container for dried foodstuffs.
A large flake or blade blank thinned on both faces to form an approximate leaf shape. Precise function unknown.
An object with a broad enough blade which catches the wind and so is pushed until it shows the direction the wind is blowing in.

A stone core with a conical form and a core platform which may or may not be prepared. Blades are removed around the circumference of the platform. The end of the core opposite the platform usually tapers to a point. (Source: SARC)

A small stone core with a conical form and a single core platform which may or may not be prepared. Microblades are removed form the circumference of the platform. The end opposite the platform usually tapers to a point. (Source: SARC)

A core which has two opposing platforms. One end serving as the main platform for the removal of blades - the other being used to straighten the face of the core and correct knapping errors. Removals are taken from the full circumference. (Source: SARC)
The piece of stone or flint remaining after blades have been produced.
A container for the storage and or use of cosmetics.
A container for kohl, a powder usually made of antimony, used as eye shadow.
A large pot used for boiling.
A specific form of cauldron, generally of rounded profile made in the Low Countries. (Source: MPRG)
Hook used to support cloth on a tenter frame after fulling to dry the cloth.
The cutting part of a drill. Often bits are interchangeable.
A sculpture on top of a tomb etc.
An ancient Egyptian gem cut in the shape of a scarab beetle and engraved with symbols on its flat side.
A straight sided drinking vessel, usually with a wide mouth and without handles.
A beaker adapted by having a pedestal base. The upper part of the pedestal is solid and may be thrown as an integral part of the vessel, or added as a separate element.(Source: MPRG)
A by-product created during the manufacture of a tool.
A by-product of the production of microliths.
A band of material worn around the neck, waist or shoulder.
A strung object used to produce sound usually from a string instrument eg a violin bow.
A three dimensional representation.
A piece of furniture with one or more doors concealing storage space.
A container for one or more candles.
A candlestick in which a simple socket is set in the centre of a drip tray or saucer. There may be a vertical loop handle linking the socket to the rim of the saucer. (Source: MPRG)
