A candlestick in which the socket is above a drip tray or grease tray, placed on a pedestal base which is generally hollow. There may be a vertical loop handle and one or more secondary drip trays. (Source: MPRG)
A type of bowl designed to be suspended, usually from the roof.

An object which serves as a vessel. This class includes containers for which the function is either specific or unknown. Where the function is known, the term is also included within another class, eg churn.
Armour designed to protect the foot.
An instrument where the note is made by air being blown over an edge.
A small edge blown instrument with no finger holes, producing one note.
Remains of the wood seeds of fruits such as apples, pears, grapes or citrus.

The fertilized ovules of a plant, often occurring in an archaeological context, in the form of small roundish bodies or grains. Seeds are dispersed naturally or collected for sowing to produce a new generation of plants.
Armour made up of interlaced metal rings.
A cube of material used to make up a mosaic.
A cage for containing birds.
A portable confining enclosure or framework.
A large vessel with raised or applied cordons running horizontally on the body of the vessel.
A cutting blade with handle used for food preparation and consumption.
A small, sharp bladed knife for sharpening pens, originally sheathed, now the blades fold.
Long stole worn by Anglican clergy during a service.
A pair of round edged tools like chisels to make a groove in iron.
A large stone used to grind grain or similar.
A tool used to pound, crush or beat an object.
A stone for grinding grain.
Equipment used in the grinding of cereal to produce foodstuffs.
A tool for pounding or grinding.
A shaped stone, which could be hand held, used in combination with a saddle quern to grind or rub grain.
A flat based stone used in conjunction with a grinding stone
Wind-blown sand.
A wind-blown deposit.
A projectile fired from a bow.
An object which is propelled by force away from a launcher, which can include from a human.
A hole or opening in a ship's hull to allow light and air to enter the vessel, or to aim a weapon through.
A flake that has had small flakes removed to blunt, sharpen, refine the outline or prepare the edge of the tool.
A container used to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.

A shallow ceramic or metal libation bowl, usually with no handles or feet. The Greeks called it a phiale, the Romans a patera. Used in religious practice. Could contain mixed wine and water but also unmixed wine, honey, oil, water, or milk.
A bag containing food hung around a horse's head.
An object used to aid pouring a substance through a small opening.
Deposits that accumulate at the bottom of a slope, at a break in a slope, or where a barrier is encountered.
A long spear used on horseback.
A long wooden shafted weapon tipped with a metal point.
A long spear designed to be thrown in order to injure or kill a given target.
A long shafted weapon with a pointed head used for thrusting or as a missile.
A reinforced and padded item of clothing designed to protect the upper body in sports associated with physical contact or possible injury from sporting equipment. For military uses use BREASPLATE.
Clothing designed to protect the wearer from physical hazards.

A mask worn over the face to protect the wearer from inhaling airborne pollutants and toxic gases. The mask forms a sealed cover over the nose and mouth, but may also cover the eyes and other vulnerable soft tissues of the face.
A container, capable of being carried by hand, often for carrying such items as clothes, keys, money, etc.
A purse, sometimes of bronze, usually worn on the forearm.
A small bag or pouch for carrying money, especially coins.
A war trumpet, usually Iron Age.
A pin worn to hold two or more pieces of material together, often when being worn as clothing.
A set of two or more pins joined together by a chain.
A container for cooking.
Containers used for cooking.
A leaf or diamond-shaped arrowhead with shallow retouching at the edges.
An arrowhead with its base hollowed to allow it to fit onto the shaft of an arrow rather than having a tang that fits into the shaft.
The weapon end of an arrow, often shaped for particular uses.
An arrowhead which has a straight cutting edge.
A small barrel.
Armour formed from thin pieces of metal fastened together or on leather or some strong woven material.
A metal disc before it is stamped to become a coin.
An object designed to add curls to a wig.
A bowl adapted by the addition of three or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl adapted by the addition of two handles, generally opposed. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl with a single loop handle crossing over the top of the vessel from one side of the rim to the other. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl adapted by the addition of one or more handles. The handles may be vertical or horizontal loops, or straight, or may comprise vertical or horizontal lugs. (Source: MPRG)
A round container open at the top, usually to hold liquids.

A bowl with a straight handle projecting horizontally or diagonally upwards from the rim, particularly or completely hollow, but not opening into the vessel. A jar adapted by the addition of one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl adapted by the addition of a single handle
A cup offered as a prize in a contest.
A long piece of timber, thicker than a board.
A bowl with straight sides which are at an approximate right angle to the base, i.e. a roughly cylindrical shape. (Source: MPRG)
A container for holding water that has been blessed by a priest and is used for symbolic rituals of purification.
Any non structural fitting on a vehicle.
A mounting or fixture attached to a cart.
An object used in washing or cleaning the body.
A small brush used fro cleaning teeth.
A jug with a maximum diameter in the lower half of the vessel and with no discernible shoulder. (Source: MPRG)
A flat stone, often sandstone, used for paving.
A general tool used to beat objects with.
An tool used to separate flax fibres from the wood and bark following retting.
A funerary vehicle used to carry a coffin from a church or funeral home to a cemetery.

A specific form of rounded jug with a wide mouth, a large parrot-beak spout and three handles in the form of wide straps that spring from the rim to the girth.The spout may have a lug applied to the outside. (Source: MPRG)
A craft of uncertain function over 30 metres long.
A bowl with straight sides which turn outwards, forming an obtuse angle with the base, i.e. and inverted, truncated, conical shape. (Source: MPRG)
A wide-mouthed, narrow based flared bowl associated with dairying activities. (Source: MPRG)
Large granite stone used as the base stone of a stamping mill against which ore was crushed. Contains distinctive striations from the crushing process.
Equipment used to compress or break through compression.
A thin strip of material used for binding.
Wind-blown silt.
A slim piece of wood used to ignite fires, lamps, ovens etc.
A container holding materials used in writing.
A pot for ink usually housed in a hole in a desk.
A flat blade used to beat the weft against the newly woven cloth in weaving.
An object designed to bring bad luck, divine wrath, disease etc upon the bearer or target of the curse.
A wooden, or wooden soled, shoe.
A tablet, often made of lead, on which curses are written. Sometimes the tablet is transfixed with a nail.
A jar adapted by the addition of a straight handle and three feet. Profiles vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)

A jar adapted by the addition of one straight handle, attached to either rim or body, and usually with a lip at right angles to the handle. The body is generally rounded, with a short neck and an everted rim. (Source: MPRG)
A container used to measure liquids.
A measure for liquid holding two pints which is a quarter of a gallon.
A vessel, usually made of stone, which contains the consecrated water for baptism.
A measure for liquid, usually for a quarter of a pint.
A mould in which a sword is cast.
Decorative armour used for parades and display, not for combat.
A long handled shallow pan used for frying.
A shoe fixed to an animal, either permanently or temporarily, to protect the hoof and provide improved grip.
Thinner and narrower than a horse shoe these are also rarer as donkeys tend to be shod less often than horses.
Knapped or ground stone/flint tool where the specific type is unknown. Use more specific type where known.
A very small flint or other stone tool.
An axehead of flint or other stone commonly used for Neolithic types.

A stone axe in which the surface is fully covered by negative removals. Core axes may be made on flakes and have part of the flake's original surface but this cannot be part of the edge otherwise use FLAKED AXE. (Source: SARC)
A mould in which an axehead is cast.

Objects used in, or which are created as part of, a manufacturing or production process, including the by-products of such processes. This does not include finished items, these will appear in other appropriate classes.

Low-grade cast iron in the form of balls or spheres, produced in small workshops and exported across the North Sea and Baltic region. Particularly associated with 16th century Sweden. Also spelt "oszemundt" and other variants.
A device in the form of a loop with a flat base hung on either side of a saddle to support the rider's foot.
A piece of material worn as a band around the arm.
A stringed instrument with a fingerboard which has frets on it.
A stringed instrument usually with a triangular frame.
An instrument played mainly by plucking the strings. Use narrower term where known.
A plucked string instrument of a specific medieval / early post medieval type.
A plucked string instrument in which the strings pass over the soundbox and parallel to the sides of the instrument.
A stringed instrument with a very rounded body.
A small, narrow boat with pointed ends propelled by paddling.
An axehead of flint or other stone commonly used for Neolithic types.
Any form of fastener used to fasten clothing together.
Deposits formed under conditions present adjacent to ice masses during glacial periods.
Deposits formed by the mass downward movement of surface deposits over underlying frozen ground under periglacial conditions.
A Roman wine cup with a shallow bowl and a circular foot.
A basket for the storage and transport of fishing equipment. For the storage and transport of fish use creel.
A piece of furniture containing shelves, cupboards or drawers for storage or display.
A screen used to decorate or protect an opening.
A simple instrument where two pieces of material are manipulated to knock together.
A specific type of hand held hollowed wooden clapper, known in Europe from medieval times onwards.
An engraved stamp used to impress an image onto another object.
Equipment used to create objects through the use of a pattern or matrix which shapes the object.
A bottle with a narrow neck used for wine, oil etc.
A circular flask with one or two flattened faces. (Source: MPRG)
A fully automatic mounted or portable firearm, usually designed to fire bullets in quick succession from an ammunition belt or magazine.
A device for holding a lamp in a fixed position. These could take many forms from a simple bracket fixed to a wall to an onrate candelabra.
An iron striking plate used to create sparks to ignite a fire.
Objects used for, or associated with, the production of heat and light.
A container used to hold ammunition.
A pipe used for smoking tobacco.
A jar with one or more lips added on the outside of the vessel wall over a hole pierced through the wall. The lips do not usually rise above rim level. (Source: MPRG)
A handled tool with two or more prongs used for digging, cultivation etc.
Large shallow dish or plate.
Traces of food observed adhering to, or found within, an object or another material.
A coloured fluid used for writing, drawing and printing.
A small blade for moving earth, applying liquids such as plaster, smoothing etc.
A container for sand which was used to stop ink from spreading.
A round, oval or rectangular straight-sided dish, divided into two or more units or compartments of varying sizes by vertical slab partitions. (Source: MPRG)
A container used for holding or serving food, especially an open shallow container.
A form of handgun.

A pistol fired by use of a flintlock.A gunflint is held in the lock which on pulling the trigger pushes back the pan cover creating sparks which set off the powder in the pan causing a flame to go through and set off the charge in the barrel.
A chisel ended flaked axehead common in the Mesolithic.
A pouch for containing tobacco.
A container for the storage of tobacco.
A long hollow tube used to blow molten glass into shapes.
An unfinished, roughly shaped axehead.
An object used to pick the teeth with.
A thick fabric cover used to provide warmth.
Soft items used to make a bed ready to be slept upon.
A body suspended so as to be free to swing, especially one regulating the work of a clock.
A container for perfume.
The remains of a manufacturing process.

In the glass industry, material spilled or raked from the top of the crucible during melting:important for assessing the methods of glassmaking and a common find on furnace sites. Roman period onwards.
The debris resulting from the production of glass.
A tool used for splitting straw. The straw splitter would be pushed down the hollow shaft of a piece of straw to produce six flat lengths of straw ready for plaiting. Used in straw hat making.
Implement with a shallow bowl at the end of a handle used to eat with or to stir food etc.
Short-range naval weapon with a low muzzle velocity.
A weapon where an object is propelled away from its launcher by means of force. The launcher can be a weapon, eg a catapult, or a person. This term includes the projectiles themselves.
A heavy artillery piece consisting of a metal tube mounted on a gun carriage.
A heavy, smooth bore, long barrelled weapon, fired from the shoulder.
A musket fired by a piece of burning match being held in the lock which, when the trigger was pulled, would contact with powder in the pan to set off the charge.
A toothed comb used to beat the weft against the cloth in weaving.
A single seat with back and arms and usually upholstered.
A single seat with a back, sometimes also arms.
A container for ointments or perfume.

A jug with a pierced bunghole just above the base. the bunghole can be made through an applied knob or disc of clay, or simply a hole in the vessel wall. In use, the hole would probably have had a tap or spigot attached. (Source: MPRG)
A place on which a fire is made.
Polished stone balls interpreted as ritual objects in the Scottish Iron Age.
A knife handled at both ends used to shave wood.
A low, wide, open container.
A balance with unequal arms where the contents of a pan on the short arm are weighed by a weight or weights attached to the long arm.
A balance measuring weight by the tension of a spring.
A balance for weighing coins.
An apparatus for weighing, usually consists of a beam on a pivot with a means of supporting the object to be weighed on one side and weights on the other.
A discoidal flint tool often has a ground edge.
A tool used to filter solid material from liquid by means of a mesh, net or sheet with holes.
A shallow tray or dish used for baking food.
An instrument in which air is made to vibrate by a cane reed.
A composite instrument in which air is forced through one or more drones and chanter from an inflated bag or bladder.
A rigid holder for a bladed weapon.
A handled tool with a broad blade with slightly raised sides used for scooping or shovelling.
A security device attached to objects.
A metal pin, often with a screw thread and used in conjunction with a nut.
A device used to lock another object.
A ring fixed to a vehicle through which the reins were passed.
Metalwork attaching or attached to a harness.
A portable medieval container usually cylindrical or barrel shaped.
A costrel with a barrel-shaped body and a centrally placed neck. One end is usually flat and the other domed. Two opposed strap handles or lugs join the rim or neck and shoulder. (Source: MPRG)
A costrel with a narrow-mouth and two opposed handles or lugs on the shoulder.
A costrel with a cylindrical body and a centrally placed neck. One end is usually domed and the other flat. Two opposed strap handles or lugs may join the rim or neck and shoulder. (Source: MPRG)

A costrel with a flattened spherical form: the back is flat and the front domed or mammiform. The base may be slightly flattened or rounded. Two opposed strap handles or lugs join the rim or neck and shoulder. (Source: MPRG)
Tongs used with incense.
Protective covering for personal defence, objects designed to kill, injure or overwhelm an opponent or quarry, and ancillary equipment.
Four iron spikes joined together so that when the caltrop is on the ground a spike is always pointing upwards. Used to disable infantry and cavalry.
A jar with vertical concave body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A concave sided jar made in tin-glazed earthenware. (Source: MPRG)
A weapon, usually blunt, used to strike an opponent.
A hammer designed to fight with, often has a hammer side and a spiked side.
A beaker with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A beaker with a construction above the base to form a waisted profile. (Source: MPRG)
A straight sided drinking vessel, usually with a wide mouth and without handles.
A beaker adapted by having a pedestal base. The upper part of the pedestal is solid and may be thrown as an integral part of the vessel, or added as a separate element.(Source: MPRG)
A vessel comprising two or more dishes, generally of small size, joined together. There may be one or more handles of varying forms. (Source: MPRG)
An object with a ceremonial or symbolic function. Multiple index with specific object where possible, eg for a ceremonial battleaxe index as ceremonial object and battleaxe.
A term for a drinking vessel, although its use often implies a recognised quantity.
A container used to assist in cooling wine.
A container designed to assist in cooling foodstuffs or liquids. Use specific type where known.
A piece of wood or iron to secure ropes, etc to or on.
The case containing the charge for a firearm.

A stone core which is irregular in shape. It can have numerous usuable or abandoned striking platforms. It is the most common core type and it is often the last attempt of a knapper to extract the final usable flakes form the core. (Source: SARC)
An extra sole on a shoe.
Fine-grained, non-humified, organic mud formed under water. Principally composed of plant material, typically of faecal origin.
Use for deposits formed in lakes or ponds.
A miniature lead phial worn around the neck as a religious pilgrim sign, generally medieval.
A round bottomed container to be held in the palm of the hand.
An instrument for writing or drawing, which usually consists of a thin rod of graphite surrounded by a wooden cylinder.
A square, wooden drinking vessel.
An object designed to hold food for birds.

A wide, shallow, straight-sided, circular DISH, with concentric divisions, formed by vertical partitions, or by concentric grooving around the base. The use is uncertain but the name implies it was designed to hold feed for domestic fowl. (Source: MPRG)
The lower surface of a room.
An item designed to cover, decorate or enhance a wall.
Large wall hung tapestries only.
Material hung from walls, excluding paper.
Glass that has been coloured by fusing glass with a metallic oxide film or by burning pigment into the surface.
Glass used within a window, usually to protect from the elements, can permit a view through it or can be for decorative purposes.
Fittings for animal harness.
Polished bone used to burnish objects eg pottery.
A shallow, usually circular, dish on which food is served or eaten from.
A small container used for dispensing table salt for seasoning purposes.
A jar with roughly vertical body walls which in some cases may be slightly convex. (Source: MPRG)
A vessel in which incense is burnt.
A vase used on the altar.
Weights used on a type of vertical loom to hold the warp threads taut.
A vessel with heating apparatus beneath it, for cooking or keeping food warm at the table.
A handheld serrated device for grooming horses.
Equipment used in the care and upkeep of animals, usually used directly on the animal.
A Roman wine cup with a shallow bowl and a circular foot.
A microlith retouched to a half moon or crescent shape. (Source: SARC)

A jar with a single handle attached to the rim edge and looped over the top of the vessel. There may be a lip. Profiles can vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
An object which has been rejected eg due to being misshapen, spoilt etc.
A by-product created through a manufacturing process. This does not include the production of tools, for this use tool by product.
An object made of sponge used to wash or clean with.
A large one handled drinking vessel, sometimes fitted with a hinged lid.
A small shaped container designed to bathe the eye.
Objects used for the preparation of medicines or the diagnosis and treatment of ill health or disease.
Remains of the animal group Crustacea: hard-shelled arthropods which are largely aquatic. Use for the remains of shrimps, prawns, crabs and lobsters, and also woodlice.
Group of fresh water crustaceans which include the water fleas (Daphnia ssp.) the egg cases (ephippia) of which are found in archaeological deposits.
Material derived from living things. Use more specific term where known.
Material of unknown origin observed adhering to, or found within, an object of another material.
A lid with a shallow domed profile and a central knob. (Source: MPRG)
A tool used to impart an image, impression or mark onto a surface.
A stamp used to emboss paper.
Equipment used to create a mark, image or character in an object.
A tool used to notch, line cut or scratch into a surface.

A jug form which usually has a short, narrow neck and rim and an applied spout. The spout is usually tubular and free standing, or attached to the rim/neck, rising from just above the high shoulder or girth. (Source: MPRG)
Remains of any part of a hazel (Corylus avellana L.) nut.
Remains of any part of a nut including the nutshell. The definition of nut as used here is any woody fruit or seed.
Containers used to serve food and drink. Use appropriate term.
Objects used for the preparation of food and drink,for cooking and consumption.
An object used to mark each mile and to indicate distances to locations.
A container in which substances are heated to high temperatures.
A cup adapted by the addition of a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A vessel for keeping the ashes of the dead after cremation.
A bowl used to wash in.
A German stoneware form of conical mug. (Source: MPRG)
A mug with more-or-less straight side which flare out from the rim to the wider base. (Source: MPRG)
A scraper with retouch at right angles to the bulb of percussion.
A flake or blade with retouch along one or more edges.
A flint blade or flake with retouch on one or both ends.
A small semi circular scraper current in the Bronze Age.
A flat tile with raised or flanged edges, used with imbrex for roofing.
A wooden rod passed through a spindle whorl. Used to handspin fibre.
A pipe in which air is made to vibrate by blowing across an edge, including transverse flutes and recorders.
A bowl with a sharp change of angle in the vessel wall, generally at the point of maximum diameter. the carination may be in the upper or lower half of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)
A brush used in writing.
A stone with a concave upper surface on which grain was ground by using a rubbing stone.
A long scarf or shawl.
A border put around a picture to enhance it and ususally to hold in place the picture and any covering, eg glass.

Derived from the Colchester in the mid to late 1st century, particularly in south and east Britain. Characterized by the attachment of the separate spring and pin through two holes in a lug behind the head, which projects to form a crest atop the bow.
A ring, flange or short piece of pipe, made of iron, clay or other materials, used to join and seal two sections of water pipe.
A fitting of unknown function. Use more specific type where known.
A small, double-handled bowl with horizontal loop handles decorated with a cockerel motif. (Source: MPRG)
The object used to make impressions in wax as seals.
An ancient Egyptian gem cut in the shape of a scarab beetle and engraved with symbols on its flat side.
A form of handgun.
A gun designed to be held in one hand. It has a short barrel length compared to long guns.
A flask with a hollow ring-shaped form. (Source: MPRG)
A large fork with wide spaces between its tines, used to throw grain etc into a breeze to separate grain from chaff.
The engine housed within the fuselage of an aircraft that powers its flight.
A machine consisting of a number of moving parts designed to produce a specific physical effect, such as the powering of a craft or other device.
A tool to apply material to the seams of a ship.
An instrument used for surgery, which is the treatment of injuries by instruments and manually.
A pointed two-edged surgical knife.
A covering for the foot not enclosing the ankle.
A defensive or protective covering for the head.
A type of helmet with a hinged visor.
A rod or spindle, either fixed or rotating, on which a wheel or group of wheels is fixed.
Armoured covering to protect the hand and wrist.
A container to hold a liquid in honour of a deity.
A lever lock key.
A machine for creating bails of straw.
A small bar raised or lowered by a latchlifter which is used to fasten gates or doors etc.
A vessel used in 'dry cupping' to form a vacuum over an area of skin.
A musket with a snaphance lock. This lock consisted of a flint and steel but the pan cover was separate to the steel unlike the flintlock.
A container for drinking, with a base and stem but without handles.
The blade or sharp-pointed head attached to the end of a spear's shaft.
A type of spearhead with a triangular blade bevelled at the edges and with a groove(s) following the outline.

Type of leaf-shaped socketed spearhead with two small holes or loops on either side at the base of the blade. Possibly meant to assist in securing the metal spearhead to the wooden shaft, or to tie streamers of some kind to the top of the spear.

A spearhead with an elongated hollow cast into the base of the blade to receive the end of the wooden spear shaft. The spearhead was secured to the shaft by pegs set at right angles or by lashings fixed to loops cast at the base of the spearhead.
A hand held device with bristles or hairs used for cleaning or painting.
A brush for applying paint.
A vessel comprising of two or more bowls, generally of small size, joined together. There may be one or more handles of varying forms. (Source: MPRG)
A nodule of iron pyrites used to create fire in combination with tinder and flint or another nodule of iron pyrites.
A part of a musical instrument played by the feet.
A stone with the date on it, sometimes of an event, person etc.
An object which has words inscribed, engraved carved or written upon it.
A child's bed, usually without rockers.
A short hooded cloak.
A mine designed specifically to be dropped from an aircraft, often into the sea.
A container packed with explosives, with a detonator triggered by contact or a timer, for the purpose of destroying enemy craft, personnel etc.
A pendant in the shape of a cross.
An instrument used to create and link loops of yarn to produce a garment.
A brick made of clay which is difficult to fuse, used in boilers and fireplaces.
An oblong mass of hardened clay or mud, used in building.
A hook with a spiked tang which allows it to be driven into a vertical surface.
An object which has been bent and angled from which something else can be caught or suspended.
A rod used to measure the imperial measurements of a pole or a perch.
A vessel containing a religious oil or unguent.
A large shallow bowl for letting liquids stand.
A vessel used to produce butter from milk through agitation.
A large container with lip and handle usually for milk.
A container used to catch blood during bleeding.
An ornamental decoration which projects from the surface it is part of.
A barrier used to close an opening, usually hinged and often hung within a supporting frame.
A thin board forming part of a musical instrument and placed in a position to strengthen or increase its sound.
A bench with a back, usually in a church.
A measure for liquid holding two pints which is a quarter of a gallon.
A container used to measure liquids.
A thin, pointed implement used for sewing with a hole at the blunt end for threading the sewing material.
A needle for making and mending nets, often forked at both ends.
A needle used to sew together thatch.
Type of footwear which covers a minimum of the foot and ankle.
An object fashioned in the image of a penis.
A bit which scoops out material.
An instrument where vibrating air is confined by a tube. Use narrower terms where known.
An instrument in which air is made to vibrate by a cane reed, which may be single or double, and may or may not be contained in a reed cap.
The cover for a garderobe on which a person would sit.
A cylinder containing a piston by which fluid can be ejected or sucked in.
A mug with more-or-less straight side which flare out from the rim to the wider base. (Source: MPRG)
A German stoneware form of conical mug. (Source: MPRG)
A piece of furniture containing a chamber pot or wash basin.
A small box or chest for valuables, especially jewels.
A grooved ring holding a gem or semi-precious stone.
An instrument composed of several pipes in which air is made to vibrate by blowing across the top edge.
Light material used to cover the face or head.
An instrument strung with metal pieces which sound when shaken.
A hollow instrument containing material that produces sound when shaken.
A figurative or decorative object made of corn, used as a charm for luck or to ward off evil spirits.
An object which when hot is used to smooth fabric.
Pieces of bone used in a game, the pieces are thrown and then picked up between bounces of a small ball or throws of another piece.
A wooden framework with holes for the feet, and occassionally hands, in which the offender was restrained.
A clip used to attach pieces of harness together.
A medieval wind instrument.
A vertical structure usually made of stone, brick, timber, etc.
Glass which has been coloured and decorated by painting.
A jar with a profile that slopes inwards from base to rim. Particularly shallow examples have been term Peat Pots; this is a term with a restricted distribution in northern England. (Source: MPRG)
A case for the transportation and protection of musical instruments.
A cigar shaped implement used to release knots and tangles in weaving.
The impost, or point at which a vault rests upon its support, and from which it seems to spring. Hence the term describes the bottom stone of a vault, which lies on the impost.
A bowl adapted by the addition of two handles, generally opposed. (Source: MPRG)
A small, double-handled bowl with horizontal loop handles decorated with a cockerel motif. (Source: MPRG)
A long handled weapon which has a long blade in line with the shaft, sharpened on both sides and ending in a point.
A mortar used specifically for grinding cosmetics.
An Anglo-Saxon brooch type dating from the 5th to 7th centuries AD and influenced by the Crossbow brooch, featuring a sprung pin and a head plate.
A small, often of about two inches or less in length, version of the Long Brooch usually made of less expensive materials than other brooch types and frequently worn in pairs.
A weight used to sink a fishing net in water.
A weight used to sink a fishing line or a net in water.
A weight attached to a fishing line in order to help it sink towards the bottom.
A gaming piece for the Viking game of Hnefatafl.
Movable joint attached to fixtures and fittings to enable opening and closure of eg a door.
A hinge composed of two parts: a u-shaped hinge attached to a door which slotted over an L-shaped staple in the door jamb.

A metal, often decorative, hinge constructed with a single large strap attached to the surface of the moving part wiht a loop in the end which fits over a pintle attached to the adjacent stationary frame.
A wheeled passenger vehicle, especially one with four wheels and pulled by horses.
A small hole for a lace, cord or hook to be passed through.
A solid ball fired from cannons, size and weight would depend on the calibre of the cannon.
A woven fabric used to trim clothes.
A device with a handle and cutting edge to help remove skin from vegetables and fruit.
Animal remains bearing evidence, such as knife marks on bones, that they were slaughtered and used for food.
The remains of any part of any animal. Use specific term where possible.
Microscopic animal remains. Use more specific term if known.

An astronomical instrument for telling the time at night. The date is set on the nocturnal then its pointers lined up with the Pole Star and the two primary stars in the Great Bear, thus allowing an approximate time to be read off.
A stone coffin.
A writing material prepared from the pithy stem of an aquatic plant.

A convex, usually round, piece of metal or other material mounted at the centre of a shield. Originally serving a practical purpose in deflecting blows and providing a mounting for the grip, it later developed into an ornamental feature.
A carved or moulded decorative projection adorning an object or, in the case of architectural features, the termination of a moulding or the intersection of ribs or beams.
An object made of two verticals with rungs between for ascending or descending.
A mounting or fitting attached to a chariot.
A dish comprising three or more lobes, usually equally spaced. There may be one or more animal or other figures inside the base and two or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A small shallow cup used in assaying gold or silver.
A bolt for fastening metal plates. One end is put through prepared holes and then beaten flat.
A drinking vessel, made of glass, designed expressly to be used for the consumption of wine.
A tool which combines two or more different types of tool.
A small disc or flat ring of metal, wood or leather.
Clasp for fastening together two loose ends.
Metal shaped blade that flares out to a curved cutting edge, with a side loop to further assist hafting.

A bronze axehead of middle or late Bronze Age date in which the side flanges and the bar/stop on both faces are connected, forming a single hafting aid. For palstaves with side loops use looped palstave.
A knife designed to be used for pruning plants.
A dish adapted by the addition of a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A mixing or grinding vessel.
A small metal ring through which a nail or rivet is passed and fixed.
A container, typically cylindrical with a narrow neck, usually for containing liquids.
A vessel for the storage and transport of wine.
A key for winding a clockwork mechanism.
A square or cylindrical pipe at the top of a chimney which helps the draught of air.
A shackle for the feet.
A restraining ring usually linked to another.
Any by-product which cannot be reused.
A roller used in the fulling process to felt cloth.
A bowl used to contain and burn incense.
An object used to burn incense in.
A container for incense.
A container for incense.
An object used to prick holes on each side of a manuscript page to mark the postion of the horizontal lines.
A long spear used on horseback.
A large vessel, sometimes large enough to accomodate several gallons, often made of leather and richly decorated, for storing and serving liquor.
A tube for the transport of liquids and gases eg for drainage purposes.
A pipe allowing the passage of water through it and designed only for this.
A mowing implement with a large curved blade and a long handle.
A stone inscribed or marked with ogham letters used for a memorial
A container to provide a set amount of gun powder.
Attachments that form part of the bridle.
A metal mouth piece used to control an animal, usually used on a horse.
A metal, shankless bit, with a ring on either side used to control an animal (usually a horse) by exerting direct pressure on the tongue, lips and the side of the mouth.
A book of hymns.
A bound collection of paper or other materials.
A tool used for weeding and breaking up the ground prior to planting.
A covering for the arm, usually attached to or part of another garment.
The base to a door or window frame or of a wall.
Flexible, twisted fibres, joined together, for tying objects.
A tube through which missiles are blown.
A leather working tool.
A container to provide a set amount of gun powder.
Molluscs found in fresh or brackish water habitats.
skos:Concept.skos:prefLabel: Fresh And Brackish Water Mollusca Remains

skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: Molluscs found in terrestrial habitats as opposed to fresh or brackish water.
Molluscs found in dry to damp habitats rather than fully aquatic or marine habitats. Use more specific term where known.
Straps fastened around the legs of a hawk.
A tool used to examine or explore a cavity eg surgically for exploring the depth and direction of wounds.
A bowl used in magical ceremonies.
An object representing the sun, made of bronze or gold usually dating from the European Bronze Age.

A hinged plate of brass, iron or wood inboard of a ship's scuttle or porthole which can be lowered and secured with a butterfly nut to prevent damage to glass in heavy weather or mask internal light from enemy vessels.
An object attached to a window which is removable.
A flag or object depicting a symbolic representation carried as part of a religious ceremony.
A wooden framework with holes for the head and hands, in which the offender stood.

Use for the remains of internal parasites of vertebrates, including humans, such as flukes, tapeworm, including their calcified cysts, etc. Eggs of the nematodes Ascaris sp. (maw-worm) and Trichuris sp. (whipworm) are commonly reported.
Use for ticks and true mites. Related to spiders, mites are widely present in archaeological deposits but are rarely studies in detail as they can prove difficult to identify.
Remains of arthropods characterized as having a body divided into two segements and four pairs of legs, such as spiders, scorpions, ticks and mites.
A shade for a window.
An item hung over a window, usually can be open or shut to allow light in.
A shafthole adze with additional hammer knob, normally polished stone.
A fishing spear with two heads to grip fish between.
A long pointed weapon used to catch fish by stabbing.
An instrument using lenses to magnify the object being viewed.
A specimen of material to represent the whole from which the sample was taken.
A sample consisting of minerals, stones etc.
A sample taken from waterlogged conditions.
A flint tool for piercing holes.
A small narrow pointed missile that is thrown or shot.
A fragment of the body of a vessel, or hollow object, usually of ceramic or glass. If known, use specific vessel type.
A pattern cut out in wood or metal to help shape something accurately or to reproduce shapes.
A small container for holding oil, vinegar, salt etc for use at the table.
A thin flat board or plate usually with a raised edge on which things can be carried.
A collared urn with a heavy rim or collar on top of a straight-sided or slightly bowed body narrowing to a small flat base. Decoration may extend for a short way below the collar, but this is rare.
A large vessel, sometimes large enough to accomodate several gallons, often made of leather and richly decorated, for storing and serving liquor.
A bottle used for the storage of medicine.
A container used upon the altar.
A vase used on the altar.
An item used to fasten a cloak either to another garment or to itself.
A tool for pounding or grinding.
A plug to keep out dust, moisture etc.
An object used to fill up a hole.
A flexible container with an opening at one end.
A spiked stick, the spiked tip frequently of metal, used to urge livestock forward.
A device used to throw projectiles, can vary from a small forked stick for hurling stones to a large engine of war to hurl large projectiles.
An axe used by the Saxons and Franks for throwing.
An axe designed and weighted to be thrown.
A reversible device for measuring time by the transfer of sand from upper to lower glass bulb.
A sandglass that runs for an hour.
A measure for liquid, usually for a quarter of a pint.
A specially designed chisel for producing ornamental mouldings.
A shaped stone, which could be hand held, used in combination with a saddle quern to grind or rub grain.
A pan with a perforated bottom for straining or rinsing food.
A knife used for sacrifices.

A type of flanged lid where the lower flange is partly cut away to leave two projections or lugs, which can be fitted into corresponding cut-outs in the rim of the vessel beneath and secure the lid when it is rotated. (Source: MPRG)

A lid with a distinct flange, projecting upwards, horizontally or downwards, and an integral knob. Complex examples can include a simple locking device: lugs on the lower flange can be twisted into matching cut-outs in the rim to lock it. (Source: MPRG)
Deposits laid down as the result of river action.
A dish comprising three or more lobes, usually equally spaced. There may be one or more animal or other figures inside the base and two or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A vehicle on runners for carrying loads or passengers, especially over snow. Could be drawn by horses, dogs, reindeer and/or pushed or pulled by people.
A pair of cannon balls joined by a solid bar.
A small container used to fill a lamp with fuel.
A combination of spear and axe on a long handle.
A wig, toupee or section of extra hair designed to replace or enhance the wearer's real hair giving greater quantity, bulk or length.
A specific German Stoneware form of rounded mug. (Source: MPRG)
A mug with a rounded body profile below a deep neck. There may be a marked junction between the neck and the body. The neck may be inward-sloping, upright or everted. (Source: MPRG)
A stone/cobble/boulder with worn/smooth wear traces left by grinding/polishing axes/adzes.
A metal or wooden framework suspended within an opening in an external wall, fence, hedge etc, to allow ingress or egress.
A monocular optical instrument possessing magnification for observing distant objects, especially in astronomy.
A long narrow spoon used to remove cosmetics or medicines from narrow necked containers.
A handheld lamp used to send morse code messages ship-to-ship or ship-to-shore.The lamp and its reflector can be covered/uncovered by a trigger operated shutter.
Objects used to transmit a definite message through a visual non written means, includes stamps and matrices to create these.
A stone tool, often only polished on the edge.
A bar used to increase the force being brought to bear in order to move an object.
An object in the shape of an Egyptian dung beetle, used to symbolise the motive power of the sun. Use specific term where known.
A square, wooden drinking vessel.
A very small spoon used to provide liquid to mix with ink on an ink stone.
A weapon with a long shaft and a three pronged head.
A tool used to smooth wood. It has a flat surface with an adjustable blade protruding through it.
An object fixed to the door or floor to limit the amount a door can open.
A form of frying pan, usually medieval.
A material resulting from the smelting of iron ore.
A band worn around the leg to hold up a sock or stocking.
Clothes worn against the skin and beneath other clothes.
A bead used to create a space or interval within a necklace.
A melon shaped bead.
A small, pierced, often spherical or cylindrical, piece of glass, wood,etc, which may be strung together with others to form a necklace.
A stick used to beat a drum.
A short chain or chains attached to a woman's belt for carrying keys etc.
A tool used for digging the ground.
A leather working tool.
A mould used to cast coins.
A piece of material, usually glass, which has been ground into shape to enhance vision or protect the eyes.
A blank for a knife that has not been hammered out.
Instruments used within geometry, geometrical drawing and navigational mapping.
An instrument for taking measurements and describing circles, with two straight arms connected at one end by a movable joint.
A large jug or pitcher with a wide mouth.
A jug with a body profile resembling two cones joined together at their widest points, producing a distinct carination. (Source: MPRG)
A piece of material, eg metal, cloth, leather etc, added to a button to enhance or embellish it.

A jar adapted by the addition of two handles. Handles may be horizontal or vertical loops or horizontal or curved lugs. Handles are nearly always opposed. Profiles vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
Use for deposits formed in caves.
A bowl divided into two or more compartments by vertical slab partitions. (Source: MPRG)
Signs or badges of office, honour, membership, occupation etc.
A shaped piece of iron perforated for nails and used on horses.
A specific type of small rounded jar. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with an evenly rounded profile. (Source: MPRG)
A necklace holding charms or being itself a charm.
A long-handled implement designed to allow the user to scratch their own back.
An object which does not provide light but supports or is attached to lighting equipment.
A tube to produce drops of liquid.
One of a set, used as standards for specific coins, often bearing stamps and other marks of authenticity.
A band worn around the wrist as a bracelet.
A curving metal band attached to the underside of the stock of a firearm, vertically enclosing the trigger to prevent accidental discharge.
A clay cone, part of a furnace.
A small container used for dispensing table salt for seasoning purposes.
A container used to hold substances for dying or painting.
A box to contain the dowry which is a gift or endowment given to or on behalf of the wife.
A garment hook having a flat plate, often decorated, with two or more holes for attachment and a hooked projection. The term is often used for early Medieval examples.
An object designed to be the end of a lace or simimlar to aid the insertion of the lace through a loop or eyelet.
A hard sharp point used to cut a design into metal.
A small spherical bell for fastening onto the leg of a hawk.
A bell worn by an animal, eg sheep, goats, cows and hawks, to inform the owner of the animal's position.
A receptacle for spittle, usually used in a public place.
A metal disk with a turned rim, giving a resonant note when struck.
A microlith retouched to a thomboid shape. (Source: SARC)
A rod around which fibre is wound for spinning
A large jug, usually rounded with a narrow neck, used for holding liquids.
A tool with a pointed head for breaking ground.
A bowl with straight sides which are at an approximate right angle to the base, i.e. a roughly cylindrical shape. (Source: MPRG)
A jug with more or less straight sides that flare out from a narrow rim or neck to a wider base. (Source: MPRG)
A piece of metal or wood with a hole through which a bolt can be screwed.
A container, usually rectangular and having a removable or hinged lid.
A two panelled altar decoration that can be closed.
A lockable box for holding money and valuables.
A container designed to be difficult to get into or out of, thus ensuring the safety of objects inside the container.
An overshoe with a thick sole or set on an iron ring to raise the wearer's foot out of the mud and so protect normal shoes.
A boat fashioned out of a section of tree trunk.
A small vessel propelled on water by an engine, oars or sails.
A measure for dry substances. A quarter of a bushel.
A short stabbing weapon, designed to be fixed to the muzzle of a musket or rifle, even in some cases to pistols. Some were designed to be used both fixed to long guns and independent of them.
A bayonet designed to fit over the muzzle of a musket or rifle, allowing the gun still to be fired.
A weight used to hold down thatch.
A device with a spirally twisted shaft, pointed end and handle for removing corks from bottles.
A lid with a carinated conical profile and straight vertical sides, surmounted by a conical top with and integral knob. (Source: MPRG)
A small ornamental case, usually on a necklace or chain, that holds a picture, keepsake etc.
Any dome shaped metal bell struck either by an internal clapper or by a hammer.
A key usually with a long stem with bit, stem and terminal in line.
A tool with a sharp tapering end used for piercing materials or as a weapon point.
A bone or antler point with rows of barbs, usually on one side only.
A strip of material, leather etc used to hold two sides of an object together.
A container used to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.
A folding circular cover on a stick held in the hand for protection against rain.
A heated iron used to label, burn or mark animals, slaves, criminals etc.
An instrument for breaking open hard shelled nuts.
A screen dividing one part of a church from another.
A screen of wood or stone, used to divide the nave from the choir.
Covering for the head, especially ornamental.
A semi circular jewelled headdress for formal occasions.
Decorative ornamentation worn on the head.
A container to hold and/or apply grease.
An object designed to add curls into hair.
A wooden hammer.
A box in which one or more games are stored.
A modern piece imitating an ancient coin in an attempt to deceive, usually for financial gain.
An amulet containing a charm in the shape of a scarab.
The vertical side of a window, archway, doorway or fireplace.

A device for telling the time by the sun, using the shadow cast by a gnomon, or sometimes a spot of light through a small hole. They were often used for checking the accuracy of clocks. They range from panels on the sides of churches to pocket watch size.
A piece of armour worn to protect the throat.
The staff or crook of a bishop or abbot.
A rotating wheel, horizontal to the ground, on which pottery is made.
A piece of fabric worn tied or knotted around the neck.
A guard or cover put in front of a fire to prevent embers or flames setting fire to a building.
A spiked, iron plate fixed to a boot for climbing on ice.
A measuring rule graduated in feet.
A graduated length of material used for measuring distances.
A form of padlock with a cylindrical case and bolts and springs attached to spines.
A detachable lock with hinged loop used for attachment operated by a key.
A jar for the storage of ointment.
A specific form of cauldron, generally of rounded profile made in the Low Countries. (Source: MPRG)
A large pot used for boiling.
A vessel composed of three or more separate compartments, joined together and pierced to allow liquid to flow from one to another. There may be one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A disc >4mm with a central perforation.

A jar with a single handle attached to the rim edge and looped over the top of the vessel. There may be a lip. Profiles can vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
A tall holder for candles which stands on the floor.
Use for the remains of any part of a mammal, except for humans.
Use for Insectivores (hedgehogs, shrews, moles), Chiroptera (bats) and rodents (squirrels, rats, voles, mice, dormice).

A sailmaker's thimble used in the sewing of canvas, consisting of a flat thimble in a canvas or leather strip with a thumb hole. The whole is worn across the palm of the hand, which gives it its name.
A small cap for protecting the finger or thumb whilst sewing.
Use for deposits formed in estuaries.
A dedicated or consecrated offering.
A container used to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.
An object used in smithing to enlarge holes, consists of a tapered square rod.
A simple flat-topped lid resembling an inverted dish but with no central knob or handle. (Source: MPRG)
A stone placed across a grave.
A microlith retouched to a triangular shape. They can be sub-divided into scalene and isosceles triangles. (Source: SARC)
A cord or handle which rings a bell when pulled.
A soil, which may be buried or exposed, that has developed in a past landscape.
A tool used to sharpen another tool or object.
A revolving stone used to sharpen or polish by grinding.
A leather strap for sharpening a razor.
A stone used for sharpening edged tools, knives etc.
Equipment used to sharpen other objects.
A small pin or stud worn in the lapel, often decorative.
A projecting stud which fastens into a socket.
A bowl with concave body walls. Wide-mouthed, narrow-based examples, particularly those in larger sizes, have been termed Pans or Pancheons. (Source: MPRG)
An object designed to float in water and to support a net.
An object that supports another in water, eg float on a fishing line or net.
A jar for the storage of ointment.
A measuring container to measure dry goods.
An inscribed marker stone found on Hadrian's Wall.
A tall, thin pillar of wood, stone or metal on which a lamp could be fixed.

A musket fired by use of a flintlock. A gunflint is held in the lock which on pulling the trigger pushes back the pan cover creating sparks which set off the powder in the pan causing a flame to go through and set off the charge in the barrel.
A pipe used for smoking where the smoke passes through water.
A bottle for the confinement of evil spirits.

A type of dish specifically designed to catch the juices from roasting meat. The dish may be oval, semi-circular, rectangular or wedge-shaped. Usually has one or two handles located on one long side and a lip at one or both short sides. (Source: MPRG)
A horn used to hold loose gun powder for recharging firearms.
A mug with little or no discernible neck and an even, convex profile from rim to base, which have roughly equal diameters. (Source: MPRG)
Implement with a shallow bowl at the end of a handle used to eat with or to stir food etc.
Hand held instruments which assist in the holding, cutting and eating of food.
A hand held utensil with two or more prongs to hold food.
A bayonet designed to plug into the muzzle of a musket or rifle.
Right angled triangular plate for determining, measuring or setting out set angles.
A wood or metal dowel for joining objects together eg for securing roofing slates.
A tool used to reduce the diameter of metal rods. Consists of a pair of semicylindrical surfaces.
A round bottomed container to be held in the palm of the hand.
Part of the glass making process, the frit is broken up into a crucible and heated to create glass.

Simple organisms, mostly single celled, that move by means of two flagella (complex cellular projections). Most live as marine plankton, but some occur in fresh water. Members of the Kingdom Protista.
A large container for holding or storing liquids.
A bowl with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A long vertical pillar usually of stone used as a structural support in buildings often artistically sculpted or shaped.
A method of grinding cereals where one stone is rotated on another by hand.
An axe used by the Saxons and Franks for throwing.
An axe designed and weighted to be thrown.
A pointed projectile fired from a crossbow designed to injure or kill a given target.
A container for ointments or perfume.
An auger bit which creates its hole by drilling into the material.
A tool with a cross handle at one end and a spoon shaped or gouge cutter at the other, used for drilling holes.
A bracelet or necklace formed by hammering gold onto a triangular-sectioned bar and twisting the metal to form a spiral pattern.
A necklace or armband made of metal.
A knife designed to be used for pruning plants.
An object which fastens cross ways.
A container with a perforated bottom through which material is shaken or poured.
Material consisting of at least 90% organic matter by dry weight. Formed from plant material under damp, wet or acid conditions where the rate of accumulation is greater than the rate of decay.
A long handled heavy hammer used by two hands.
A chequered board used to play chess.
An example from which another object is made.
A specific form of globular jar with a rounded or very sagging base and an inturned, generally simple, rim. (Source: MPRG)
A narrow container, especially one in which food or water for animals is put.
A band of material, often leather, or a chain or similar worn around the neck of an animal for identification and or restraint. Where this is part of harness use the specific harness term.
Container with arched handle used for carrying liquids, foodstuffs etc.
An object on which thread or yarn is wound or hooked.
A bobbin where thread is wound around it, used for example in spinning and weaving
A slate tablet, often framed in wood, for writing purposes. Use a materials thesaurus to record the material type slate.
A pressure vessel used to store gasses at above atmospheric pressure.
The burnt remains of a corpse.
A medical container for collecting urine for study.
A deep, rectangular or oval, straight-sided vessel with an everted, crenellated rim, used to hold either wax for dipping candles or molten sulphur for dipping matches. (Source: MPRG)
An iron rod with both ends sharpened and turned down, used for holding timbers together.
A three sided or U-shaped metal fastener on which the ends are pointed.
A type of staple used to hold two object together.
A jar used to store medicines, drugs or the constituent parts of medicines and drugs.
A sharp, pointed metal object.
A type of spike, often on candlesticks.
A large container used for washing the body.
A tablet with a handle fitted to the back of it.
A three panelled altar decoration that can be closed.
A small tool with a shovel blade and a pick blade set at right angles to the handle.
Unsorted sediment (i.e. containing everything from clay to boulders) laid down directly by a glacier.
Deposits formed as the result of the action of ice masses during glacial periods.
A horizontal pierced metal object upon which to hold the coals of a fire.
The part of the bridle that fits around the horses head.
A framed instrument, in which strings are plucked or hit, operated from a keyboard.
A circular flask with one or two flattened faces. (Source: MPRG)
A bottle with a narrow neck used for wine, oil etc.
A cylindrical container, usually bulging outward in the middle and usually held together by metal hoops.
A device to hold and rotate food against a heat source during cooking.
An instrument used to compress a substance into a smoking pipe.
A piece of furniture intended to hold drinks, side dishes etc.
A stone grooved on one face thought to have been used to straighten and smooth arrow shafts.
A wooden or metal object used to provide a rhythm by striking another object.
A stone which bears an inscription commemorating an event or person.
A vehicle with two wheels for carrying loads.
Use for deposits formed by the action of the sea or under marine conditions.
A ring containing a scarab shape.
A small explosive device, usually hand thrown.
A tool used in etching which is the use of acid to breakdown parts of a metal plate in order to create an image.
A vessel with a specially adapted mouthpiece, to assist in feeding young animals and humans.

A bowl with a straight handle projecting horizontally or diagonally upwards from the rim, particularly or completely hollow, but not opening into the vessel. A jar adapted by the addition of one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A pair of cannon balls joined by a chain.
A type of chain shot characterized by a number of chains attached to the main shot, causing maximum damage. Sometimes known as a Spider shot.

A form of axehead where the body is hollow allowing for insertion of the haft. Usually bronze or iron and of late Bronze Age or Iron Age date. Most examples feature a side loop to further assist hafting.
A large bag made of coarse cloth or thick paper.
A flat level structure, can be part of a series, used to allow a person to move from one level to another, or can occur singularly on a threshold.
An instrument for measuring height above sea level.
A cup adapted by the addition of a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A container used as a lavatory by bedridden people.
A tool used to produce designs in plaster on the outside of buildings.

A jar adapted by the addition of two handles. Handles may be horizontal or vertical loops or horizontal or curved lugs. Handles are nearly always opposed. Profiles vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
Breast ornament
A ring worn on a toe.
A component of reciprocating engines - the moving component that is contained by a cylinder.

A bowl with a straight handle projecting horizontally or diagonally upwards from the rim, particularly or completely hollow, but not opening into the vessel. A jar adapted by the addition of one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
An apparatus worked by hand or mechanically for weaving yarn into textile.
A jug with an evenly rounded profile and a discernible basal angle; the maximum diameter is near the mid point and in the upper half of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)

A specific form of rounded jug with a wide mouth, a large parrot-beak spout and three handles in the form of wide straps that spring from the rim to the girth.The spout may have a lug applied to the outside. (Source: MPRG)
A metal spur fitted to the leg of a fighting cock as an aid in fighting.
A small tool which pierces material. It has a flat cutting edge.
A small carved or sculpted figure.
A tray for separating grain from chaff.
A rope, cord etc used to hold or secure animals.
A musical instrument with a toothed cylinder.
A decorative container for serving cream.
A scale or index of a sextant, astrolabe or similar instrument.
A carved or moulded decorative projection adorning an object or, in the case of architectural features, the termination of a moulding or the intersection of ribs or beams.

A convex, usually round, piece of metal or other material mounted at the centre of a shield. Originally serving a practical purpose in deflecting blows and providing a mounting for the grip, it later developed into an ornamental feature.
A circular band, usually metal, worn on the finger for ornamentation.
Armour designed to protect the leg or parts of the leg. For specific foot protection use FOOT GUARD
A bar or rod, usually of metal, used to push another object into place eg to push coals within a fire.
A wall plate used by insurance companies to identify insured property.
This includes all articles created to be used for personal care, hygiene, grooming and cosmetic activities.
Piece of armour worn to protect the back, often permanently connected to the breastplate.
A tool used for weeding and breaking up the ground prior to planting.
A metal reinforcer worn on the sole or heel of a boot.
Objects used or associated with the transmission of ideas and information through the written medium.
Mountings, often decorative, attached to a book.
An object which encloses a light source but allows light out through holes, glass etc.
A dish adapted by the addition of a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
Roughly spherical, > 5 µm, soot particles produced from the incomplete combustion of fossil fuels. Used as an indicator of industrialisation, and can also be used as a dating tool.
A flat surface supported by legs used for many purposes.
A rough table usually for working upon.
A flat edged blade used in leatherworking
A decorative fitting of mid to late 11th century date, exclusively of copper alloy, and mostly zoomorphic. Arranged in pairs, on each stirrup either side of the footplate.
A form of dagger.
A piece of cloth worn over the head.

A vessel used as the lower part of a distillation unit consisting of a round-bottomed vessel with walls sloping inwards towards the rim. Some examples may have an external flange to support the upper unit (ALEMBIC). (Source: MPRG)
A piece of wood scored across with notches for the items of an account and then split into halves of which each party kept one.
A stick inscribed with a prayer or prayers for use in praying.
A chair fixed to the end of a plank which was pivoted to allow the incumbent of the chair to be ducked into water as a punishment.
A mug with little or no discernible neck and an even, convex profile from rim to base, which have roughly equal diameters. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl used in the marriage ceremony.
An axe with a cutting edge and a hammer head.
A heated iron used to label, burn or mark animals, slaves, criminals etc.
An artifical head of hair worn to disguise baldness, for adornment, for ceremonial or theatrical use etc.
A container in which dice are shaken and from which dice are thrown.
A staff of office or authority.
A pin or bolt, especially one on which some other object turns.
A type of fabric often used to trim clothing.
A grave or sepulchre including a monument.
Equipment used to perform circumcision.
A knife that is used for circumcision.
A rack to hold smoking pipes.
A strip of cloth used for binding a wound.
A vessel with a lid, handle and spout used particularly for boiling water.
A handled tool with two or more prongs used for digging, cultivation etc.
A curfew with a rounded profile merging into a rounded top, with no discernible angle. (Source: MPRG)
An enclosed container to hold coals or logs of a fire.
A heating apparatus in which coals or embers are placed in the pedestal or base below a container in which food or liquid could be held and kept warm.
A mug with roughly vertical body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A covering for the leg which fits closely.
An object worked on a lathe, usually the bi-product of such work.
A vessel with heating apparatus beneath it, for cooking or keeping food warm at the table.
A load carrying horizontal structural member.

A jar-shaped vessel with pierced apertures or cut-out on an open or closed pedestal base. Herbs, spices or scented woods would be placed in the pot and a burning coal placed underneath to warm the herbs so they would release their scent.
Stone used to line a furnace or oven.
A tile used within a floor.
A stick for digging up roots etc. Sometimes a stone weight is attached.
A flat based stone used in conjunction with a grinding stone
A measuring rule not graduated in feet.
A small type of seat, usually without a back, set on legs.
A tool used in the separation of fibres from the bark in flax working.
A two or three wheeled motor vehicle.
Use for the remains of any part of a bird.
A long spear with a double barb where one barb is longer than the other.
A long spear designed to be thrown in order to injure or kill a given target.
Any part of an aircraft. Use more specific term where known.
The engine housed within the fuselage of an aircraft that powers its flight.
A curfew with straight sides flaring outwards from the top. (Source: MPRG)
Characteristic waste flakes struck off in the production of axes.
A tear shaped container.
A graving tool used for engraving objects. Prehistoric versions are usually made of flint or other stone, later versions usually of metal.
A container for the storage of rice.
A container for the storing of substances.
A small pierced disc which glitters, used as ornamentation on clothing
A bottle for the storage and transportation of milk.
An encircling ornament for the head, usually denoting sovereignty.
A sharp pointed nail with a large flat head.
A bar or rod, usually of metal, used to push another object into place eg to push coals within a fire.
A stopper made of cork used to prevent liquid escaping from a container.
A early match made of a piece of wood and an inflammable substance, lit by friction between the inflammable substance and a rough surface.
The fittings or parts on a strap.
A fitting specifically functioning as the strengthener and support for the end of a strap of leather or fabric.
A side scraper with a short concavity retouched into an edge. This may be achieved by a single blow. (Source: SARC)

A type of dish specifically designed to catch the juices from roasting meat. The dish may be oval, semi-circular, rectangular or wedge-shaped. Usually has one or two handles located on one long side and a lip at one or both short sides. (Source: MPRG)
A long, slender, flexible tube for inserting into a body cavity for the introduction or withdrawal of fluids.
An instrument for letting blood.
A bowl with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A box with a perforated top used for perfume.
A projection on the under side of a hinged choir stall seat providing support during long periods of standing.
A case or container for an amulet.
A collar, usually metal, used to signify slavery and to inhibit freedom, usually with a chain attached.
The windlass used to wind rope around to raise and lower the bucket in a well.
A Mesolithic type of mace head made of stone.
A tray on which an offering is made.
A pair of tongs used for placing coals onto a fire.
A pillow or cushion for lace making.
A form of hat, usually does not cover the forehead nor have a brim, and usually worn by women.
A hinged or looped clasp that fits over a staple and is secured by a pin, bolt or padlock.
A scroll containing prayers.
A muzzle loaded explosive projectile fired from a mortar. Typically they are short range and low velocity with a high arcing trajectory.

A coin struck, cast or stamped imitating an official coinage issue of contemporary or slightly later time period. The piece may consist of a similar alloy or a base core with plating of varying thickness.
A device for winding and casting a fishing line, consisting of a revolving spool with a handle attached to a fishing rod.
A metal ring which joins the jess to the leash. The owner's name is often on the vervel.
A substantial piece of timber which is part of, and integral to, the structure of a building.
An ornament for the ear, usually clipped to the lobe or fastened through a hole pierced in the lobe.
A specific type of early medieval pin. Do not use in instances where a pin has become detached from the brooch.
A bowl adapted by the addition of a short tubular spout applied just below the rim.
A small, handled axe.
A case designed to hold a fan.
The part of footwear that touches the ground.
The vertical recessed face of a pediment, often decorated with sculpture.
A short blade set at an angle in a slot between two handles.
A bowl with no noticeable base angle, so that the walls and base form a continuous rounded curve (Source: MPRG)
A large jug or pitcher with a wide mouth.
A strong vehicle with four wheels for carrying loads, usually horse or ox drawn.
A portable timepiece capable of being worn on the wrist or carried in a pocket.
A smooth bore weapon usually for firing shot as opposed to bullets.
A playing piece for draughts
A book containing prayers.
A document or book written by hand.
A large container for milk.
Armour made up segmentally, usually of bands of material.
A small cup for holding a boiled egg upright while it is being eaten.
A flat surface often with drawers used for working upon.
A flat board with short spikes used to comb out the staple of wool to remove tangles before spinning. Usually used in pairs.
Microscopic living things, usually unicellular, occasionally multi-cellular. They lack cell nuclei.
Hanging drapery used to cover an opening eg a window, often decorative.
A machine for cutting down cereal crops.
A container made of interwoven strips of pliable materials, such as cane or reed, often carried by a handle.
A small, heavy object used for keeping loose papers in place.
A drinking bowl, often wooden.
A shaft sunk into the ground to reach the water level.
A cylinder of fur or cloth into which the hands are placed to keep warm.
A small bottle for liquids.
A device for containing food whilst cooking it by the action of steam.
A wickerwork basket for holding fish or catching lobsters.
A neck band or necklace worn tightly around the throat.
A ring with separated, often ornamented, terminals.
A flake that has had small flakes removed to blunt, sharpen, refine the outline or prepare the edge of the tool.
A flake of stone struck from the core where the length is less than twice the width.
A large portable container used for carrying items such as clothes, papers etc.
A tool used in processing the fibres from coconuts.
A triangle of metal which, when struck, produces a note.

A blade attached to a shoe to glide on ice. It can be made of bone, and examples are known from at least as early as the Bronze Age. Metal blades have progressively replaced bone skates since the sixteenth century.
A small bowl on a pedestal base, which may be solid or at least partly hollow. It may have a wick lip at the rim or a lug handle. (Source: MPRG)
A container for ointments or perfume.
A ring shaped brooch, commonly Anglo-Saxon and medieval.
A navigational device.
A bowl comprising three or more equally spaced lobes. There may be one or more moulded animals or other figures inside the base and two or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl adapted by having a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A container for pills or tablets.
A rod to lift the odd numbered warp threads to enable the weft to be passed through.
A small knife used in surgery.

A jar adapted by the addition of three or more vertical loop handles. They may be equally spaced, or confined to one section of the circumference. Profile vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
Container with a handle, spout and narrow neck.
A crosspiece fastened over the necks of two or more animals, often oxen, and attached to the plough or cart to be drawn.
A religious receptacle, especially Jewish where it contains four Hebrew texts worn during morning prayers.
Remains of parasitic or free-living worms of the phylum Nematoda, characterized as having slender, cylindrical unsegmented bodies. Cysts of Heterodera commonly occur archaeologically
A spout allowing water to spill away from a building. Usually carved in images of animals, people or grotesques.
A storage container designed to contain corn.
A flake produced from a carefully prepared core.
A long shaft topped by an axe head. Differs from a battleaxe by the length of the shaft.
A stone used to test how genuine an object is by rubbing the object against the stone, particularly used for testing gold and silver.
A long staff, occasionally bound with metal, used as a weapon.
The hood used to completely cover a hawk's head.
A jar adapted by the addition of a single handle. Profiles vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
A two pronged spear for catching eels.
The sculpted head, shoulders and breast of a person.
A concave glass dish used to hold material for observation or experimentation; so named for its resemblance to the disc covering the face of a watch.
A form of compass, sometimes with a screw for setting to small intervals.
A long graduated staff used in surveying to help measure distance or height.
A complex or ornate lid which cannot be easily classified according to the main forms of lid. (Source: MPRG)
A device for catching eagles, falcons and hawks.
An object used to catch animals.
A shackle for the hand.
A short squat gun or cannon used to test the strength of coarser blasting powders. Mainly 18th-19th centuries.
A gun designed to fire shells, etc at a high angle.
A form of whip or lash, often used for self mortification.
A kind of scourge.
A thin, flat rectangular piece of slate, or similar material, used as a roofing tile.
A cloth or leather tunic, usually worn underneath metal armour, although sometimes worn by itself.
A tool with a curved blade used for pruning.
A flat bottomed drinking vessel with no handle or stem.
A microlith retouched to a trapezium shape. (Source: SARC)
A plasterer's tool for smoothing the surface of plaster.
A device for separating grain from chaff by means of a wind or current of air.
A concave sided jar made in tin-glazed earthenware. (Source: MPRG)
A large jug, usually rounded with a narrow neck, used for holding liquids.
A bowl adapted by the addition of two handles, generally opposed. (Source: MPRG)
A small, double-handled bowl with horizontal loop handles decorated with a cockerel motif. (Source: MPRG)
An instrument for measuring angles, usually in the form of a graduated semi-circle.
A term for a drinking vessel, although its use often implies a recognised quantity.
A string of beads used to aid the memory in reciting a set of devotions.

A bronze axehead of earlier Bronze Age date the sides of which are raised, either by casting or hammering, to form flanges on both faces which aid hafting. Some types also feature a raised bar or stop between the flanges.
A triangular sectioned rod probably used for making sparks.
A small doll controlled by the operators hand either directly or with strings.
A rock-like deposit consisting largely of calcium carbonate, formed by the precipitation of calcium carbonate out of calcium-rich water bodies such as springs.
A piece of fabric for wiping the nose, face or hands.
A piece of metal, usually in the form of a disc, struck or cast with an inscription or device to commemorate an event, or awarded as a mark of distinction.
A jar whose profile resembles two truncated cones joined together at the widest point, forming a carination at the centre of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)
A conical container, usually made of earthenware, into which boiling sugar was poured and allowed to crystallize.
An Egyptian religious statuette.
A head cover which can be attached to a piece of outerwear, eg a coat, or can be separate.

A specific type of hook used in textile production, also known as a sheer board hook. Harbicks were used in pairs to secure a cloth to a cropping board so that the nap (of the cloth) could be sheared.
A fine net worn over the hair.
An object used to hold offerings to a deity.
A container to hold a liquid in honour of a deity.
A case in which a pipe would be held to protect it.
A container holding a set quantity used specifically for measuring grains or cereal.
A container for holding ink.
A large strong case or box used to contain objects for storage or transport.
A form of stopper used to regulate the flow of liquid, often used in a barrel.
A cup with sides falring outwards from the base towards the rim, usually with a single handle.
The debris resulting from metal working processes.
T shaped piece of bronze imitating a key and hung from a chatelaine.
A tap inserted into a barrel to allow liquid to be drawn from it.
A tool used to examine or explore a cavity eg surgically for exploring the depth and direction of wounds.
One or more ropes or straps attached to an animal's head to provide guidance and control.
A tool used to hit fabric, eg to remove dust or dirt.
A toothed, handled tool used to drag objects either to gather them together eg grass or hay, or to level them out eg sand or earth.
A fastener used within a cavity wall to hold the two sides together.
A tool used to pound, crush or beat an object.
Remains of the group of animals characterized as being radially symmetrical in form, such as sea urchins, sea cucumbers and starfish.
Rendering material containing lime and sand spread on walls as a pliable mixture before drying to a hard surface.
A piece of equipment fastened on an animal for carrying loads, including riders, or for assisting in traction.
