An astonomical instrument, and navigational aid, in the form of one eighth of a circle and used to measure the angular altitude of heavenly bodies. Invented in 1731 and used into the 19th Century, it was superceded by the sextant.
A jug with a sinuous profile where the rim, girth and base are of roughly equal diameter, and the neck and lower body are narrower.(Source: MPRG)
A device allowing a variable amount of air through in a single direction which varies the note produced.
A portable timepiece capable of being worn on the wrist or carried in a pocket.
An indicator of the extent of an area of land.
An indicator of the extent and or ownership of a property.
A container in which weapons are put for storage and or transportation.
Use for the remains of any part of an amphibian.
The tool used to create a mark on pottery by the potter.
A knife used to rub grease into a hide and to force dirt out of it. The knife is symmetrical with a handle at each end.
Part of an instrument that is used to modify or limit the sound of a note.
A brush for applying paint.
All linen used at the table, including tablecloths.
A large, flat dish.
An object used to grip together objects.
A cup with a rounded body beneath a deep neck. There may be a marked junction between neck and body and the angle of the neck may be inward-sloping, upright or everted. (Source: MPRG)
A weight used for the measurement of wool.
Line used with hooks attached to it in order to catch fish.
Weight used with any kind of balance.
A fully automatic mounted or portable firearm, usually designed to fire bullets in quick succession from an ammunition belt or magazine.
A moulded object.
An object that carries the weft in a loom without the need to be passed from hand to hand. The thread is wound into the shuttle and can be used in hand or machine looms
A small semi circular scraper current in the Bronze Age.
A plate used during circumcision.
A flag or object depicting a symbolic representation carried as part of a religious ceremony.
A plate on which an offering is made.
A deep, square or rectangular, flat slab of clay, the upper surface of which has two or more cup-shaped holders cut into it. (Source: MPRG)
A vessel used as an ornament, often to hold flowers.
A hooked blade used to cut crops. All the blade is to one side of the handle rather than being on both sides as in a sickle.
A diagram or drawing made by projection onto a horizontal plane, often of a building.
A fitting incorporating a series of overlapping flaps providing ventilation or transmission of sound.
A tool used for digging where the blade is at right angles to the handle.
A tube for conveying the voice from one room or building to another.

A covering of thin copper plates secured, on top of a layer of tar and horsehair, to the bottom of wooden ships to protect the hull against weeds, barnacles and especially Teredo navalis, a wood-boring worm prevalent in tropical seas.
The part of a musical instrument in which notes resonate and increase the sound of the instrument.
A long stick or rod used as an aid in walking, climbing etc. Where it is a weapon use quarterstaff or staff weapon. Where there is another use, use the appropriate term eg surveying staff.
A decorative band or feature.

An ornamental (carved and/or painted) frieze carved into, or attached to, the planking either side of a vessel's bow carrying the vessel's name and often illustrating some aspect of the name, port/country of origin etc.
A stone blade with one edge blunted.
Ornamentation fixed to a horse's harness.
A car adapted for ease of use by people with disabilities.
A vehicle used to transport passengers. Cars usually have four wheels and an internal combustion engine.
A container in which needles are held.
A circular or sub circular brooch with an attached pin. There is a gap in the circular part of the brooch.
A dish with straight or convex sides that turn inwards, forming an acute basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A small tool for boring holes with a cross handle and a screw tip.
An enclosed cage used in a mine shaft to transport miners from the surface to the mine workings.

A fastener made of a metal circle connected to a metal loop fixed to an object and used to fasten to another object by means of a button or a loop. The usage of these items is unclear, could include use for animals or for dress.

A wide, shallow, straight-sided, circular DISH, with concentric divisions, formed by vertical partitions, or by concentric grooving around the base. The use is uncertain but the name implies it was designed to hold feed for domestic fowl. (Source: MPRG)
A by-product usually from the smelting process.
A core with more than two distinct platforms usually including one or two striking surfaces and the the traces of former platforms. Usually restricted to flake production. (Source: SARC)
A sawn piece of timber, thinner than a plank and often rectangular.
Residue resulting from the presence of sewage. While you can have a cesspit, it contains sewage not cess.
An object that has been cut off.
A bowl with no noticeable base angle, so that the walls and base form a continuous rounded curve (Source: MPRG)
An object used to retrieve birds used in hawking. Consists of feathers tied to a rope, food is contained in the lure whilst training the birds.
A playing piece for the medieval game of table.
A toy in the image of a human being.
A thin flat board or plate usually with a raised edge on which things can be carried.
A specialised fitting for Roman armour. For a part of a garment use APRON.
A frame set with teeth used to drag over ploughed fields to break down the earth clods etc.

A pistol fired by use of a flintlock.A gunflint is held in the lock which on pulling the trigger pushes back the pan cover creating sparks which set off the powder in the pan causing a flame to go through and set off the charge in the barrel.
A band or bracelet worn around the arm.
A small dish, often with a handle, for soup, porridge etc.
A blunt headed instrument used for striking objects.
A box containing tinder, any dry inflammable material, usually also contains flint and steel for lighting fires.
Decorative fitting hung from harness.
A bowl with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A small containment for drugs which can be ingested.
A knife designed to be used for pruning plants.
An opening within a structure to allow air and light in and or to allow a sight outside or inside.
The front piece of horse armour.
Armour used to protect a horse.
An object to point at words whilst reading, eg the Alfred jewel.
A round, oval or rectangular straight-sided dish, divided into two or more units or compartments of varying sizes by vertical slab partitions. (Source: MPRG)
A shaped and or carved stone.
A low stool for resting feet upon.
A bowl whose form resembles two cones joined together at their widest points, forming a carination.
Any instrument for plucking or strumming a string eg a small piece of wood or plastic used to strum a guitar.
A gun using an explosive propellant to fire sharpened metal shafts, usually fired through water.
A cup with sides falring outwards from the base towards the rim, usually with a single handle.
A light weight umbrella used as a sun shade.
Container with a handle, spout and narrow neck.
A container for drinking, with a base and stem but without handles.
A flake or blade tool with more than one notch on its edge.
A tool with one or more serrated edges.
An enclosed, round bell with a slit, sounded by a loose internal pellet.
A vehicle containing a couch shut in by curtains and carried by men or beasts of burden.
A pillar, rod, pin, or plate of a sundial, showing time by its shadow.
A container, usually round and deep.
A loose cloak.
A badge, usually of lead alloy, distributed at various religious shrines of the medieval period and worn on clothing as proof of a pilgrimage and as a talisman.
A specific form of cauldron, generally of rounded profile made in the Low Countries. (Source: MPRG)
A utensil used to pour a liquid in honour of a deity.
A rod used to create repousee work on metal vessels. The rod is Z shaped.
A flail used for the mortification of the flesh.
A tool used to polish the surface of an artefact.
A trinket worn as a protection against evil.
A flat bottomed drinking vessel with no handle or stem.
A strap from the reins to the girth of a horse, preventing it from raising its head too high.
A small, sharp bladed knife for sharpening pens, originally sheathed, now the blades fold.
A tube, cyclinder or similar casing enclosing the mechanism of a pump.
A wheel with a groove in it for a rope or cord, used to raise heavy objects.
A detached vertical structure acting as support to a superstructure. Where a pillar is a separate monument, use a monument type thesaurus.
A piece of strengthened material worn projecting over the forehead and eyes to provide shade for the eyes.
A set of small pincers used for gripping and plucking.
A cup with a marked change of angle in the body wall, generally at the point of maximum girth. (Source: MPRG)
A drinking bowl, often wooden.
The top stone of an arch which holds it all in place.

A type of stoneware jug dating to the 16th and 17th centuries, popularly known as a Bellarmine jug, shaped in the figure of a bearded man. They were produced in the Cologne region, particularly Frechen.
A key, usually with a long stem, its terminal is generally hooked or looped, and bit set at angle to stem with radiating or looped wards.
Volcanic ash.
A residue left as the result of the burning of material.
A book containing prayers.
A metal receptacle used for burning coals, charcoal etc to provide heat.
Ornamental band or chain worn around the arm or wrist.
A missile consisting of a number of weights joined together by cord. The bolas entangles the legs of a target causing it to fall.
A system of communication used in a house to summon servants or on board ship to transmit orders to the engine room.
A pot for fine powder.
A narrow flake which is the by-product of making a burin.
Curved, interlocking roof tile of S-shaped section usually made of clay or concrete.
Outer casing of an explosive cartridge fired from a cannon or mortar.
A knife with a hinged blade which folds into a slot in the handle.
A weapon for shooting arrows.
A large, powerful, hand-drawn bow, usually 6 ft (1.8m) in length and traditionally made of wood.
A tool used for digging the ground.
A bowl with a sharp change of angle in the vessel wall, generally at the point of maximum diameter. the carination may be in the upper or lower half of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)
A tool for boring holes.
A drill where the bit is twisted by means of a bow with the string wound around the shaft of the drill.
A shaped rod which passed through a hole in the door and was used to raise a latch or to slide a bolt.
A device with two flat plates and a mechanism used to force the plates together, to help solidify milk products in cheese making.
A bar fixed across a frame at an angle to stiffen it.
A barbed spear head used to catch marine life, usually has a rope or line attached to it.
A long pillow, especially one used as an under pillow.
An object which is attached to or connected with a door.
A water-tight cover that closes and opening in a vessel's deck used for the ingress and egress of persons and cargo.
A tool, usually metal, with roughened surfaces in the form of very small teeth.
A container, in the form of an urn, for keeping the ashes of the dead after cremation.
A large, flattish basket used in winnowing the chaff from the grain.

A collared urn with a heavy rim or collar on top of a concave neck or cavetto zone, which may or may not be decorated, and this in turn leads down to a straight-sided but narrowing body. Decoration may extend below this cavetto zone, but this is rare.
An object for touching or pricking an animal's sides to increase control of it, usually used with horses.
A type of spur with the goad in the form of a rotating disc.
A papal document.
A piece of written or printed matter providing a record of events, agreements, ownership or identification.
A kind of stone used in making ink.
A bowl with straight sides which are at an approximate right angle to the base, i.e. a roughly cylindrical shape. (Source: MPRG)
A table of months and days in the year.
A long shaft topped by an axe head. Differs from a battleaxe by the length of the shaft.
A smooth bore weapon usually for firing shot as opposed to bullets.
A bowl in which an offering is made.
A decorative fitting of mid to late 11th century date, mainly of copper alloy, often traingular or trapezoidal in shape. Mounted at the junction of the stirrup and stirrup strap.

A form of cup which incorporates a joke or conundrum, where cut-outs or holes in the body make conventional use impossible. The puzzle is how to drink from it without getting wet; usually achieved by sucking through a hollow rim or handle. (Source: MPRG)
A bottle for the storage and transportation of milk.
A tool with an edged head and a wooden shaft, used for a variety of purposes.
A modern piece imitating an ancient coin, such as a replica sold in a museum.
An axehead of flint or other stone, commonly used for Neolithic types.
A machine for mechanically separating the grain from the straw and husks.
A twisted fibre which is immersed in wax, grease or oil except for one end which is lit.
A long wooden shafted weapon tipped with a metal point.
A container used in the distilling process to catch the distilled liquid.
A drinking vessel, usually curved in shape, fashioned out of animal tusk or horn and sometimes with bands of decoration.
A pick with a point at one end and a cutting blade at the other end.

A highly decorated Anglo-Saxon Long Brooch, often made of silver or bronze, worn by women. Contemporary with the Cruciform Brooch, it features a large, square head plate and usually three rounded foot-terminals.

A pedestal lamp with a drip-tray below the bowl. There may be a vertical loop handle linking bowl and drip-tray. Some variants have a more enclosed bowl form with a small tubular spout projecting from the bowl. (Source: MPRG)
A cage specifically designed to hold insects.
A container, usually glass, used for storing medicinal substances.
A long barrelled firearm. The barrel is spirally grooved on the interior, which gives the bullet a spinning motion leading to greater accuracy over a longer range.
A device with a sharpened surface used to grate objects such, as vegetables or cheese, into smaller pieces.
A container with three legs.
A metal grid, usually iron, used to support pots over a fire.
Waste material from a kiln
A horn used in hunting to give signals.
An item with both secular and religious signifigance.
A stone missile fired from a ballista, larger in diameter than ballista shot.
A bowl adapted by the addition of three or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A hammerstone but with broad groove worked around much or all of centre to enable fixing to a handle; usually associated with mining/metalworking.
A simple, pointed implement for piercing.
Piece of work to test eventual pattern, sometimes small and used as a demonstration of crafter's skill.
A bag stuffed with soft material used to support the head whilst sleeping.
A flat, circular plate, usually made of iron, on which food items are baked.
A dish with straight or convex sides that turn inwards, forming an acute basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A hook to join parts of clothing together.
A stone heated in a fire then dropped into a liquid to heat it.
A farrier's tool for paring a horse's hoof.
A hand held weapon used to throw missiles such as stones or bullets.
A lid with a shallow conical profile and an integral knob. (Source: MPRG)
A solid, spherical projectile, originally made of stone but later iron.
Woman's fur cape for the shoulders.
A small round dish on which a drinking vessel is set.
A knife that is used for circumcision.
An attachment to a lamp.
Stone used as a building material which has been finished, or dressed, before use.
A curved roofing tile.
A large, flat dish.
A piece of armour used to protect the armpit.
A massive hammer of wood, stone or iron.
A broad container, usually used for cooking.
A piece with one or more surfaces with either/both slightly convex shape appropriate for use in forming metal artefacts.
The ending for a lace.
A circular frame with a stretched membrane and small cymbals in the frame.
A set of shelves.
A graduated vessel or instrument for gauging or measuring the force or quantity of a fluctuating object, such as rainfall, tide, wind etc.
Weight on the end of a graduated line for measuring the depth of water.
A stone inscribed or marked with runes.
A microlith retouched to a rectangular shape. (Source: SARC)

A small bowl on a pedestal base above a drip-tray or saucer. It may have a wick lip at the rim. May have pierced lugs projecting form the rim for suspension of a handle, spout or vertical loop handle linking bowl and saucer. (Source: MPRG)
A pair of uprights supporting a crosspiece. Often very decorative, fire dogs were probably used as a spit or to hold pots over the fire.
An instrument showing the direction of magnetic north and bearings from it.
A tool used in making pottery.
A specific form of globular jar with a rounded or very sagging base and an inturned, generally simple, rim. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with a rounded profile merging into a rounded base with no discernible basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A pipe designed to act as a drain or within a drain.
A large jug, usually rounded with a narrow neck, used for holding liquids.
A metal stud hammered into the sole of footwear to provide grip.
A tool with a handle and curved blade designed to cut through grass sods.
A medallion, as on a brooch or ring, with a stone which is often semi precious with a device carved in relief.
An ornamental wand or rod carried as a symbol of authority
A container used to catch blood during bleeding.
A tool, featuring a straight, horizontal handle from which extends a metal hook halfway along its length, used by a shearman (one who shears sheep) in the handling of wool.
A ring used in the marriage ceremony.
Decorative mounts on horse harness
A case in which a comb was carried.
A hook on harness to help attach items.
A container for ointments or perfume.
An object used to create a blast of air.
An end scraper which has a retouched end that is concave in form. (Source: SARC)
A small boat of wickerwork covered by a watertight material.
A thick heavy weapon used to strike the opponent.
A microlith retouched to a point, usually with oblique retouch. They are synonymous with 'obliquely blunted points' of the English Mesolithic. (Source: SARC)
A frame shaped to fit around the neck and shoulders of a person for carrying buckets. Usually made of wood.
A wedge shaped stone used in an arch.
A trinket worn as a protection against evil.
A flat object which is broad and rounded at one end and tapers to a handle at the other.
A bell worn by a cow to identify its location.
Use for the remains of any part of a reptile.
A form of dagger.
An instrument made of a hollow cylinder or hemisphere covered at one or both ends and sounded by striking.
Headgear for a horse, usually consisting of a series of buckled straps and a mouthpiece, bridle bit, by which the horse is controlled.
A metal counter used in calculation of financial sums but not itself directly exchangeable for coins, goods or services.
A trap designed and used to catch fish.
A small piece of metal used to mend ceramic, glass or metal plates and vessels.
A pot for drinking hot milk curdled with ale and flavoured with spice used as a remedy eg for colds.
Ornamentation worn specifically for formalised dancing.
A pad of cloth or fur for keeping the ears warm.
A missile consisting of a number of weights joined together by cord. The bolas entangles the legs of a target causing it to fall.
An object made to hurl a spear further and faster than possible by hand.
A glass vessel with a long tapering neck that is bent down. Used for distillation.
A stamp, made of clay or stone, used to mark bars of eye ointment.
An object used to preserve fish by immersing the fish in smoke.
A shoe of soft leather.
Use specific form where known.
An iron bar used as a lever.
Soft coverings and associated objects for walls, furniture, floors and windows. Also items such as chairs, beds, and cupboards.
An attachment to furniture that can be detached.
An object assumed to be to pick limpets out of their shells.
Headwear where a band of material is wound around a cap.
A flask with a rounded, almost spherical shape. (Source: MPRG)
A case which holds and protects a mirror.
A small shovel used with incense.
A container, usually square or rectangular, used in metal processing for separating metals one from another, usually silver from gold.
A metal basket, mounted on a pole, in which oil or pitch was burned for illumination.
A mould which is used to provide the shape of a piece of footwear.
An animal skin prepared as a writing or painting surface.
An object which contains or is inscribed with spells against disease, witchcraft etc, or providing luck to the wearer.
An architectural ornament usually surmounting the apex of a gable, canopy or roof.
An ornament at the top of a roof or spire.
A wheeled open box for carrying loads.
A pan for cooking fish.
An instrument for measuring atmospheric pressure by its effects on mercury in a glass tube. It can also be used to measure altitude and depth and is especially used for predicting the weather.
An earth based plaster the mixture of which may contain clay, dung, straw, lime putty, sand or horsehair.

A stone axe in which the surface is fully covered by negative removals. Core axes may be made on flakes and have part of the flake's original surface but this cannot be part of the edge otherwise use FLAKED AXE. (Source: SARC)
A flared bowl of medium to deep depth, with wide horizontal everted rim. These vessels may have a single handle. (Source: MPRG)
A head covering, usually with a brim.
A device inserted in the ear canal to protect the wearer from loud noises or the intrusion of water, foreign bodies, dust or excessive wind.
A vessel composed of three or more separate compartments, joined together and pierced to allow liquid to flow from one to another. There may be one or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
An object which supports or is attached to a blind.
A stone tool, often only polished on the edge.
A tool for creating holes in the ground in which to plant seeds or plants.
A nail of distinctive form, specifically used in the shoeing of animals, eg horses, donkeys, oxen.
A pointed tool used to pierce holes, usually in textiles.
A bow, fixed to a stock, with a mechanism to fire the bow.
A flag or military symbolic representation on a pole.
A tray used for separating the grain from husks and straw.
A form of belt or sash worn to hold in garments.
A basket for the storage and transport of fishing equipment. For the storage and transport of fish use creel.
A cylinder containing a piston by which fluid can be ejected or sucked in.

A long, thin cane of glass made by layering glass of various colours in a mould and pulling the soft glass until the cane was of the desired thickness. When cut and viewed in section the cane has a mosaic like appearance.
A garment worn on the foot and lower leg.
A strap passing underneath the animal to hold saddle in place.
A container used to assist in cooling water.
A long barrelled firearm. The barrel is spirally grooved on the interior, which gives the bullet a spinning motion leading to greater accuracy over a longer range.
A small cup for holding a boiled egg upright while it is being eaten.
An instrument designed to measure temperature.
A pointed instrument for writing, usually on wax tablets.
Paper applied to walls.
A long tapered flexible pole used with a fishing line and usually with a fishing reel.
A post to which the offender is tied to be whipped.
A bowl adapted by having a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A jar with a roughly cylindrical or slightly flaring lower body and a rounded, inward sloping shoulder. (Source: MPRG)
A side scraper with a short concavity retouched into an edge. This may be achieved by a single blow. (Source: SARC)
Two discs at the end of a short shank, used to fasten shirt fronts, collars etc.
A religious cloth often used to cover coffins.
A small structure used to support strings on an instrument pushing them away from the body of the instrument.
A flint tool with a convex top and flat base.
A type of jug decorated with applied figures in the form of knights in armour. (Source: MPRG)
A piece of plate armour designed to protect the shoulder.
A thin scoop used for cleaning the ear.
A large stone used to grind grain or similar.
A type of retort used for distillation.
A cup with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A primitive musical instrument.
A piece of metal or timber holding two elements together.
A small knife used in surgery.
A vertical pillar or post, often shaped.
A vessel comprising of two or more bowls, generally of small size, joined together. There may be one or more handles of varying forms. (Source: MPRG)
An instrument for establishing the horizontal line of sight and used in surveying. It usually has an eyepiece and an air bubble in a glass tube or dome.

A vessel composed of three or more equally spaced lobes and usually, a waisted or pedestal base. The handles are vertical loops, generally of strap form. There may be two opposed handles, or one per lobe junction. (Source: MPRG)
An instrument with a series of pipes at different pitches, the air being produced by bellows, and pipes selected by keyboard or sliders.

A flint blade with negative impressions of removals on one side of the dorsal surface, creating a crest. These constitute part of a previously worked striking platfom or result from preparing the flaked surface on a core before detaching flakes or blades.
A quern where one stone is rotated on another to grind grain.
A simple type of plant. For most of their lifecycle they exist as haploids (i.e. only one set of genes as opposed to two). Most have clearly differentiated stems and leaves.

An end scraper which has a retouched end that is straight in form. If the tool blank is a blade or flake, and the retouch is abrupt retouch rather than scraper retouch then use either Truncated blade or Truncated flake. (Source: SARC)
A small rake used to move the embers of a fire.
An object used in combat to attack or cause damage to an opponent.
An instrument consisting of a small lyre-shaped metal frame held between the teeth, with a steel tongue plucked with the finger.
A form of temporary horseshoe, of Roman date, used when unshod animals were brought onto metalled roads. Usually consists of flat plate, winged sides and hooked heel.
A tile which covers the ridge of a roof.
An instrument, usually consisting of a disc and pointer, formerly used to make astronomical measurements, especially of the altitudes of celestial bodies and as an aid in navigation.
An associated group of toilet articles.
A thin flat slab used to mix cosmetics on.
A container for loose gun powder. Sometimes with a regulator to release enough powder for one shot.
A staff with a fork at one end which would be used to hold a lighted slow match which was used to fire a canon.

A wide, shallow, straight-sided, circular DISH, with concentric divisions, formed by vertical partitions, or by concentric grooving around the base. The use is uncertain but the name implies it was designed to hold feed for domestic fowl. (Source: MPRG)
A pipe designed for smoking opium.
A vessel consisting of three small pots joined, often on a hollow ring, at the base.
A bowl adapted by the addition of a short tubular spout applied just below the rim.
A container for keeping the ashes of the dead after cremation.
A pair of scissors used in circumcision.
Worked or unworked block/boulder/cobble, on which grain was ground by to-and-fro motion, resulting in a deep smooth elongated hollowed area.
A large container or reservoir for liquids or gases.
A large bolt or arrow fired from a ballista.
A flexible pipe, usually fixed to a pump or tap, for the conveying of water.

A jar adapted by the addition of one straight handle, attached to either rim or body, and usually with a lip at right angles to the handle. The body is generally rounded, with a short neck and an everted rim. (Source: MPRG)
A jar adapted by the addition of a straight handle and three feet. Profiles vary and should be double indexed with the appropriate JAR type. (Source: MPRG)
Ash and partially fused residues from a coal fired furnace or fire.
Lenses worn in a frame designed to improve or protect the vision of the wearer.
A stoppered bottle, for serving drinks.
A carved bust, figure or representation usually fixed to the bow of a ship.
A freestanding pole, stake etc often used to support a superstructure.
A type of spur which ends in a point, blunt or sharp, which is used to aid control of an animal eg a horse.
A key for a padlock.
A small cushion into which pins are pushed for storage.
A bowl comprising three or more equally spaced lobes. There may be one or more moulded animals or other figures inside the base and two or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
A container for pills or tablets.
A long wooden shafted weapon tipped with a metal point.
A thin piece of metal which when struck vibrates to produce a sound, can be used in pairs.
A small bag or detachable outside pocket.
A small round dish on which a drinking vessel is set.
A stone placed over or at the head or foot of a grave, or at the entrance to a tomb.
Remains of the endocarp (stone) of a member of the genus Prunus which includes sloes, plums, cherries, apricots and peaches.
A fob can consist of a chain and or a ring.
An end scraper that has a retouched end which is convex in form. (Source: SARC)
A simple, flat or nearly flat lid. In ceramic forms, often made by rolling out clay rather than by throwing on a wheel. (Source: MPRG)
A beaker with a construction above the base to form a waisted profile. (Source: MPRG)
An object made up of a wick surrounded by wax or tallow which burns slowly to provide light.

An instrument for determining, measuring or setting out right angles, usually consisting of two pieces or arms set at right angles to each other, but sometimes with the arms hinged or pivoted to measure any angle.
An Anglo-Saxon cruciform Long Brooch, often ornamented with knobs across and terminating its crossbar and head, and frequently with its foot shaped into a zoomorphic motif.
Small crustaceans ranging in size from 0.2mm to 30mm and possessing a bivalve carapace or 'shell'.
A small earthenware container used for liquids.
A large container used for the process of cleansing and thickening cloth by beating and washing.
A drinking vessel, usually curved in shape, fashioned out of animal tusk or horn and sometimes with bands of decoration.
A shallow dish or plate used to serve main meals off or to serve sweetmeats, these usually being painted with devices and riddles.
Long piece of silk etc worn around waist or over one shoulder often as a symbol of rank.
A pot for drinking hot milk curdled with ale and flavoured with spice used as a remedy eg for colds.
A small container for an aromatic substance, eg perfume.
Used for holding water and washing hands, usually in the shape of an animal.
A weight made to be hung from a steelyard.
A perforated strip of metal for shoeing the bifurcated hoof of an ox.
A net used in fishing to catch or land fish.
A pair of flat faced tools used to smooth iron.
Use type of instrument where known.
A pair of scissors used in circumcision.
A vertical structure used to draw off smoke from a fire.
An object for cleaning a pipe used for smoking.
One of a number of tiles cemented together to form pillars supporting the floor above a hypocaust.
An individual section of panelling. Can form fences, ceilings, an outer layer to a wall etc.
A machine for freeing the cotton from the plant.
An instrument for measuring time.
An end scraper made on a flake that is wider than it is long. (Source: SARC)
A device for rotating a spit.
A bowl with a single loop handle crossing over the top of the vessel from one side of the rim to the other. (Source: MPRG)
A portable writing-case or surface, especially one for use on the lap. May contain compartments for storing writing materials and paper.
A large, branched candle holder.
A vessel with heating apparatus beneath it, for cooking or keeping food warm at the table.
A flat surface designed to hold objects. For a set of shelves use shelving.
A bowl specifically used for shaving.
waste resulting from the working of wood or associated with a carpenter's workshop.

Roman mid-first to third century AD S or reverse-S shaped brooch. Often enamelled, with its central section expanded for decoration, the brooch depicted stylised creatures derived from Celtic art. The pin was looped over the neck of the brooch.
A triangular piece of wood, usually of ebony, to which the lower ends of the strings of a musical instrument are fastened.
Instruments and their component parts used to produce music or audible signals of all forms.
A fence made of pales.
A badge, usually of lead alloy, distributed at various religious shrines of the medieval period and worn on clothing as proof of a pilgrimage and as a talisman.
A pan with a long handle which would be filled with coals and drawn over sheets to warm a bed.
A stone used to crush objects.
The heraldic bearings or shield of a person, family or corporation.
A tool for threshing grain.
A weight hung on a piece of string or twine used to show the vertical.
Container with a handle, spout and narrow neck.
A metal, stone or wooden projection with a flat upper surface to support a shelf, arch, statue etc.
A stick used to bring down curses.
A stone used to cover a grave.
A close fitting garment, usually stiffened by boning or similar and tightly laced to shape and support the wearer's figure.
Instruments of psaltery and dulcimer type.
A specific type of small rounded jar. (Source: MPRG)
An open lamp which was filled with oil then a wick floated on the oil.
Large shallow dish or plate.
A piece of iron cut from blooms and lengths of bar, probably for transportation, which were then reworked.
An instrument made by inserting a stick through a membrane stretched over a hollow vessel then played by rubbing the stick.
A jug form characterized by the addition of three applied feet. The feet are usually short and stubby. Double index with the appropriate profile type. (Source: MPRG)
A flag bearing a device, often carried on two poles or hanging from a cross piece. Usually used in processions.

A distinctive form of jar being a flat-based, straight-sided or slightly rounded jar with two opposed, upright, rounded, tongue-shaped lugs raised from above the rim and bridged by added, internal struts used to suspend the pot over a fire. (Source: MPRG)
A shaped flint used in flintlocks to create the spark to fire the gun.
A measuring rule used for cloth, with the imperial yard divided into quarters and nails (sixteenths), or using the now obsolete ell of 45 inches.
A flask with a rounded, almost spherical shape. (Source: MPRG)
Written or typed correspondence, usually between two or more parties.
A decorative piece connected to the altar.
A large trough or vessel commonly created from a hollowed out tree trunk for the storage of brine prior to processing for salt.
An axehead of early Bronze Age date, made of copper or bronze, which is flat on both sides and generally lacks side flanges or other hafting aids.
In the glass industry, a flat block of stone (idealy marble), cast iron, or a ceramic material, on which cylinder glass is flattened. Medieval and Post Medieval.
A small, handled, open vessel, generally with a steep profile, designed to sit partially within a larger vessel and used to warm drinks. (Source: MPRG)
Use for the remains of an egg whether from a bird, reptile or amphibian.
A structure for hanging criminals, often used to suspend criminals after execution, consisting of one upright post with an arm.
A decorative clasp to fix sleeve cuffs together.
A container used to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.
An instrument for holding back a part of a body eg an organ.
One of the component tiles used in the construction of a tile hearth, being the floor of, or area in front of, a fireplace.
An instrument with curved arms for measuring difficult or awkward shapes.
Containers used to serve food and drink. Use appropriate term.
A jug with a roughly cylindrical or slightly flaring lower body, a pronounced inward-sloping shoulder and a marked neck/body angle. (Source: MPRG)
Equipment or tool used to compress an object into a desired form, a flatter appearance etc.
A bowl comprising three or more equally spaced lobes. There may be one or more moulded animals or other figures inside the base and two or more handles. (Source: MPRG)
An instrument to remove the seeds from flax.
A sliding metal bar, often fixed to a door or similar, which moves through staples so that its end fixes into another staple or hole which is attached to a fixed object, eg a door frame.
A large one handled drinking vessel, sometimes fitted with a hinged lid.
A close fitting skull cap, usually worn under a helmet.
A flexible armour designed to protect the torso.
A tool, possibly for cleaning mud from parts of a plough.
A bowl with concave body walls. Wide-mouthed, narrow-based examples, particularly those in larger sizes, have been termed Pans or Pancheons. (Source: MPRG)
To be used for the frame elements only, not for the bedding.
An encircling ornament for the head, usually denoting sovereignty.
Storage vessel, usually a two handled narrow necked jar.
Silver rings believed to have been used in the Hebrides and Irish Sea regions during the Viking period, and derived from Hiberno-Viking arm rings.
A device fitted and locked onto the head of a woman as a form of punishment, featuring a metal plate that fits into the mouth to impede speech..
A block, usually of iron, upon which objects are shaped and hammered eg in smithing.
An object which contains or is inscribed with spells against disease, witchcraft etc, or providing luck to the wearer.
A mug with straight sides flaring outwards from the base towards the rim. (Source: MPRG)
A light plough used to break up the earth. It does not have a mouldboard to turn the earth.
A raised carving.
A long handled fork with two or three long curved tines for tossing straw or hay.
A flattened glass sphere used to give linen a gloss.
A box used for storage or shipping.
A triangle of cloth worn by ladies to cover the shoulders, neck and throat.
A jug with straight vertical sides and roughly equal rim and base diameters. (Source: MPRG)
A pronged iron hook used in cookery.
A fragment of the rim of a vessel, or hollow object, usually of ceramic or glass. If known, use specific vessel type.
A short stabbing weapon.
Early Bronze Age assumed to be a neck ornament, crescent moon shaped, often gold and elaborately decorated.
A stick for agitating milk in a butter churn.
A tool for splitting and chopping.
A wooden cylinder or dowel used for pinning planks or timbers together.
A vessel used for medical purposes.
A residue, primarily lead oxide, formed during refining silver
A pot for ink usually housed in a hole in a desk.
A circular ornament worn on the head.

A piece of wire or similarly flexible material shaped to form a clip for fastening papers together; invented by the Norwegian Johann Vaaler in 1899. The familiar double-oval 'Gem' clip was developed by Gem Manufacturing Ltd. in England the same year.
An unofficial, usually local, substitute for coinage, which could be exchanged directly for goods or services in certain areas or with specified retailers.
A container for holding ink.
A small hemispherical bowl with a pinched or pulled wick lip. (Source: MPRG)
A brush used to disentangle hair.
An object decorated or otherwise marked by the use of a stamp; for example, a maker's stamp.
A device used to gauge the speed of a vessel at sea.
A type of cast chisel with a sharp, concave edge for shaping wood, stone or metal. A particular type of British Bronze Age tool.
A bag or case stuffed or padded, designed to be slept on.
A jug with a rounded profile and a rounded base, i.e. with no discernible basal angle. (Source: MPRG)
A roll of paper or parchment, especially with writing upon it.
A screen to intercept the heat from a fire.
A cup with a marked change of angle in the body wall, generally at the point of maximum girth. (Source: MPRG)
A container to receive milk during the milking process.
A bowl for the washing of hands, often in a religious institution.
A Roman mixing vessel with a gritted inner surface.
A contrivance of interlocking parts used to fasten objects together.
A stand or tray for ink bottles and or pens
A container to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.
Armour designed to protect the torso, and consists of both back and breast plates.
A group of coins deliberately deposited together.
A mug with straight sides flaring outwards from the base towards the rim. (Source: MPRG)
A curved blade used by the Romans and Greeks to scrape the body after bathing.
A bowl with concave body walls. Wide-mouthed, narrow-based examples, particularly those in larger sizes, have been termed Pans or Pancheons. (Source: MPRG)
A jar that contains an offering.
The barrel of a feather, usually goose, used as a pen.
A stone with a rectangular depression into which molten metal, usually tin, was poured to create ingots.
A piece of armour designed to protect the arm or a part of the arm.
A decorative panel worn over the chest and stomach by both men and women in the 16th century and later only by women.
A container for ointments or perfume.
A tripod used to support pots over a fire.
An heraldic design depicting armorial bearings.
A small bobbin used in lace making.
A container for sand which was used to stop ink from spreading.
A light made by dipping a rush in grease.
A flask with a hollow ring-shaped form. (Source: MPRG)
A mug with a rounded body profile below a deep neck. There may be a marked junction between the neck and the body. The neck may be inward-sloping, upright or everted. (Source: MPRG)
A specific German Stoneware form of rounded mug. (Source: MPRG)
An object used in the sublimation process. It is pear shaped with both ends open.
A mug with little or no discernible neck and an even, convex profile from rim to base, which have roughly equal diameters. (Source: MPRG)
A bag containing food hung around a horse's head.
An engraved object which when struck imparts the image engraved onto the leather.
A projection used to clip clothing together.
A knife prior to undergoing the processes of sharpening or finishing, consisting of a single piece of metal from which the blade and tang have been shaped.
A motor vehicle designed to transport cargo.
A beaker with convex body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A gaming piece used specifically to play chess.
A hook from which a lamp could be hung.
Algae usually found as a silica skeleton. They obtain their food by photosynthesis and are used in ecological reconstruction.
A bowl divided into two or more compartments by vertical slab partitions. (Source: MPRG)
A bowl adapted by the addition of a single handle
A bowl adapted by the addition of a single handle
A bottle used for the storage of medicine.
A narrow strip of fine material, used for trimming or tying.
A large shallow bowl for letting liquids stand.
Waste resulting from the processes associated with the making of shoes.
A horizontal beam over an opening to support a wall.
A long handled scoop usually used with liquids.
A block of metal with a device cut into it, used to impress a coin blank.
A sharp blade used for shaving.
A bowl whose form resembles two cones joined together at their widest points, forming a carination.
A tool for threshing grain.
A bent piece of metal or bone, often with a barb, which is tied to a line and used to catch fish.
A lid comprising a shallow flared dish with an integral knob rising from the centre. (Source: MPRG)
The upper part of a column or pier.
Semi precious stone or glass engraved with a design and used to make an impression on wax or clay.
A bowl whose form resembles two cones joined together at their widest points, forming a carination.
A tool for perforating or stamping, used in conjunction with a hammer.
A conspicuous ornament usually a feather or of horse hair worn attached to headwear.
Debris resulting from the manufacture of tools and other artifacts from bone.
A metal or pneumatic rubber rim to a wheel.
A tool for scooping loose substances, usually with a handle and a curved blade partially covered at the handle end.
A closed chamber used for heating or baking objects.
A tool used to remove shavings from an object.
A bowl adapted by having a pedestal base. (Source: MPRG)
A tool used to pull down burning structures.
A container used as a lavatory.
Waste products ensuing from the casting process.
A bit for an auger to scoop out material.
An instrument for measuring or drawing angles, consisting of a quarter circle, graduated to 90 degrees, with a movable arm.
An enclosed chair for carrying a person, usually carried by two people.
One of several stones used to raise a farm building, such as a granary, above the ground surface.
Usually a L or T-shaped tumbler lock key which allowed the bolt to be pulled across by a cord.
A large piece of material used on a bed for sleeping on or under.
An object placed to mark the site of a grave.
A hollow ball for containing aromatic substances eg perfume.
A piece of metal in front of a fire to stop embers rolling onto the carpet.
A ladle used for the making of drugs and medicines.
A square of fabric tied around the neck.
The original pattern/type of an object.
A stoppered bottle, for serving drinks.
A strong container, usually metal, secured for storing money or valuables.
A tool used to incise or ipress images.
A weight used with letter scales for weighing letters or parcels and establishing the required postal rate.
A large, flattish basket used in winnowing the chaff from the grain.
A large bag made of coarse cloth or thick paper.
A mould in which an ingot is made.
A tool used to impart heat in order to fuse two or more metal surfaces together.
A road vehicle designed to carry many passengers.
A written journal, often of a personal nature.
An object used in tablet weaving to control the shed.
A container with a perforated bottom through which material is shaken or poured.
A belt used to confine a hawk's wings.
A flaky or spheroidal byproduct of the iron forging process, composed of either iron oxide or magnetite.
A straight rod, made of glass, plastic or other materials, used to agitate or mix chemicals, pigments, beverages or other fluids or substances.
A stud used for fastening an item usually used for clothing.
A small flake or blade with a series of notches along the edge.
A drill bit used in metal and wood working.
A bowl with a sharp change of angle in the vessel wall, generally at the point of maximum diameter. the carination may be in the upper or lower half of the vessel. (Source: MPRG)
Any piece of timber that makes up the body of a maritime vessel, e.g. frame, decks, masts.
A lamp designed to be hung rather than to stand.
An object which surrounds or makes up a keyhole.
A bowl divided into two or more compartments by vertical slab partitions. (Source: MPRG)
A container to hold and or pour a liquid in honour of a deity.
A tool used to produce a polish on an object.
An object which supports or is attached to a curtain.
Use for the remains of any part of a fish.
Round object used to carry cable or wire
Associated group of drawing implements.
Use for deposits formed as a result of construction activities undertaken by humans or animals.
A container for ointments or perfume.
A tool with a scoop end for digging up roots etc.
A mug with roughly vertical body walls. (Source: MPRG)
A large bottle, generally protected by a basket or box, usually used for containing corrosive liquids, etc.
A thin piece of wood used as a roofing material.
A hand held tool for marking or graving.

A musket where a wheel on the lock is wound back and when the trigger is pulled the wheel moves dragging pieces of iron pyrites over the lock tocreate sparks and set powder in the pan off and so firing the main charge.
A projecting starched frill worn around the neck, especially 16th century.
A large stopper, often of cork.
A fragment of the base of a vessel, or hollow object, usually of ceramic or glass. If known, use specific vessel type.

A core used to produce flakes from a single plain platform. The base of the core, or 'keel', is often formed by alternate removal from a process resembling cresting. Often one end has a relatively unmodified section. (Source: SARC)
A musical instrument which produces its sound by being scraped.
A bowl adapted by the addition of a short tubular spout applied just below the rim.
A holder for arrows.
Glass fragments collected for re-melting. This includes not only worked fragments, but pieces broken from blowing irons and pontils, (moyles). All periods from the Roman onwards.
Protective eyewear.
A device, often consisting of a cyclinder containing a piston or plunger, used to raise or move liquids or gasses by means of compression, lifting or suction.
A flat object which is broad and rounded at one end and tapers to a handle at the other.
A staff to support a lame or legless person.
An object used to create a blast of air.
A device with hooks or claws attached to a rope and used for dragging or grasping.
A trough for containing moulding sand for use in casting metal artefacts.
Bag filled with soft stuffing used to support the body.
A measure for dry substances. A quarter of a bushel.
A long seat for reclining on.
An ornamental shoulder piece, usually a badge of military office.
A projectile discharged from a weapon, usually a firearm.
A structure consecrated for devotional purposes.

Style of early Bronze Age pot found widely in north-western Europe with a deep, largely plain, outwardly flared body above which is sharp carination, usually decorated and sometimes with an applied cordon, and an inwardly angled neck.
A knife that is used for circumcision.
Wax used to seal letters, envelopes, documents, etc.
A polished reflective surface.

An Anglo-Saxon disc brooch featuring an upturned rim. Usually decorated with zoomorphic or geometric designs either chip-carved directly on the face or applied to a thin bronze plate affixed to the brooch's floor.
A distinguishing emblem or mark, often worn to signify membership, achievement, employment etc.
A chain used to inhibit a prisoner's freedom.
An instrument in the form of a pair of pincers used to grip and pull objects, usually used in surgery.
A long spear with a double barb where one barb is longer than the other.
A protective covering to a drain.
